V012-N00001.label: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00001.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00001.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00001.conceptName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00001.preferredName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00002.label: 中医基础理论术语
V012-N00002.parentName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00002.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00002.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00003.label: 五行类
V012-N00003.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语
V012-N00003.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00004.label: 五化
V012-N00004.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00004.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00004.definitions: The five transformative states are birth, growth, transformation, harvest and storage. In the five elements, they are associated with wood, fire, earth, metal and water respectively.

V012-N00005.label: 五味
V012-N00005.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00005.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00005.definitions: The five flavours are sour, bitter, sweet, pungent and salty. In the five elements, they are associated with wood, fire, earth, metal and water respectively.

V012-N00006.label: 五方
V012-N00006.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00006.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00006.definitions: The five directions are east, west, south, north and center. In the five elements, they are associated with wood, metal, fire, water and earth respectively.

V012-N00007.label: 五时
V012-N00007.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00007.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00007.definitions: The five seasons are spring, summer, late summer, autumn and winter. In the five elements, they are associated with wood, fire, earth, metal and water respectively.

V012-N00008.label: 五气
V012-N00008.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00008.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00008.definitions: The prevailing climate qualities of the five seasons are wind, summer heat, dampness, dryness and cold. In the five elements, they are associated with wood, fire, earth, metal and water respectively.

V012-N00009.label: 五色
V012-N00009.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00009.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00009.definitions: The five colours are green, red, yellow, white and black. In the five elements, they are associated with wood, fire, earth, metal and water respectively.

V012-N00010.label: 五行
V012-N00010.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00010.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00010.englishNames: Five Phases; five elements; five phases; Five elements
V012-N00010.definitions: The interactions and changes among the five categories of phenomena represented by wood, fire, earth, metal and water.

V012-N00011.label: 五行制化
V012-N00011.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00011.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00011.englishNames: The five elements mutually control and transform
V012-N00011.definitions: A balanced, harmonized state maintained by mutual generating and restraining relationships among the five elements.

V012-N00012.label: 五行相乘
V012-N00012.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00012.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00012.englishNames: over-restriction among five elements/phases; The five elements mutually over-restrain one another
V012-N00012.pinyinNames: wu xing xiang cheng; wǔ xíng xiāng chéng
V012-N00012.definitions: Excessive restraint of an element over the element it controls.

V012-N00013.label: 五行相侮
V012-N00013.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00013.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00013.aliases: Reverse restraining/insulting among the five elements
V012-N00013.englishNames: counter-restriction among five elements/phases; The five elements mutually counter-restrain one another
V012-N00013.pinyinNames: wu xing xiang wu; wǔ xíng xiāng wǔ
V012-N00013.definitions: A restraining element becomes controlled by the element it controls.

V012-N00014.label: 五行相克
V012-N00014.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00014.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00014.aliases: Mutual controlling among the five elements； Restriction in Five Elements
V012-N00014.englishNames: restriction among five elements/phases; The five elements mutually restrain; Mutual Restriction of Five Phases
V012-N00014.pinyinNames: wu xing xiang ke; wǔ xíng xiāng kè
V012-N00014.definitions: The five elements mutually control and restrain one another. The sequential restraining relationships among the five elements are, specifically, wood restrains earth, earth restrains water, water restrains fire, fire restrains metal, and metal restrains wood.

V012-N00015.label: 五行相生
V012-N00015.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00015.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00015.aliases: Mutual generation among the five elements； Generation in Five Elements
V012-N00015.englishNames: generation of five elements/phases; The five elements mutually generate; Mutual Generation of Five Phases
V012-N00015.pinyinNames: wu xing xiang sheng; wǔ xíng xiāng shēng
V012-N00015.definitions: The five elements mutually engender and support one another. The sequential engendering and supporting relationships among the five elements are, specifically, wood generates fire, fire generates earth, earth generates metal, metal generates water and water generates wood.

V012-N00016.label: 五行胜复
V012-N00016.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00016.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00016.englishNames: The five elements overcome and retaliate
V012-N00016.definitions: The five elements have the actions of overcoming and retaliating with one another.

V012-N00017.label: 五音
V012-N00017.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00017.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00017.definitions: The five musical notes are jué (mi), zhǐ(sol), gōng (do), shāng (re), and yǔ (la). In the five elements, they are associated with wood, fire, earth, metal and water respectively.

V012-N00018.label: 土爰稼穑
V012-N00018.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00018.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00018.englishNames: earth characterized by sowing and reaping; Earth is characterized by sowing and reaping
V012-N00018.pinyinNames: tu yuan jia se; tǔ yuán jià sè
V012-N00018.definitions: The soil of the earth is where crops are sown and reaped. The earth element is used to express transformation, carrying, embracing, etc.

V012-N00019.label: 子气
V012-N00019.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00019.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00019.englishNames: child-element/phase qi; edema of legs in pregnancy; child qi; Child qi
V012-N00019.definitions: In the five elements, the qi of one element is born from the previous element.

V012-N00020.label: 子病犯母
V012-N00020.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00020.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00020.aliases: 子盗母气； Child stealing the mother’s qi
V012-N00020.definitions: In the five elements, when an illness in a child organ affects the mother organ.

V012-N00021.label: 木曰曲直
V012-N00021.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00021.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00021.englishNames: wood characterized by bending and straightening; Wood is characterized by bending and straightening
V012-N00021.pinyinNames: mu yue qu zhi; mù yuē qū zhí
V012-N00021.definitions: The nature of wood is to bend and straighten as it grows. The wood element is used to express growth, bearing upwards, effusion, free flow, etc.

V012-N00022.label: 母气
V012-N00022.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00022.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00022.englishNames: mother qi; mother-element/phase qi; Mother qi
V012-N00022.definitions: In the five elements, the qi of one element gives birth to the succeeding element.

V012-N00023.label: 母病及子
V012-N00023.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00023.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00023.definitions: In the five elements, when an illness in the mother organ is passed to the child organ.

V012-N00024.label: 水曰润下
V012-N00024.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00024.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00024.englishNames: water characterized by moistening and descending; Water is characterized by moistening and descending
V012-N00024.pinyinNames: shui yue run xia; shuǐ yuē rùn xià
V012-N00024.definitions: The nature of water is to be moist and flow downwards. The water element is used to express coldness, moistening, downward movement, storing, etc.

V012-N00025.label: 火曰炎上
V012-N00025.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00025.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00025.englishNames: fire characterized by flaring up; Fire is characterized by flaming upward
V012-N00025.pinyinNames: huo yue yan shang; huǒ yuē yán shàng
V012-N00025.definitions: The nature of fire is to flame upwards. The fire element is used to express warmth, rising, flourishing, etc.

V012-N00026.label: 金曰从革
V012-N00026.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00026.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00026.englishNames: metal characterized by changing; Metal is characterized by yielding and changing
V012-N00026.pinyinNames: jin yue cong ge; jīn yuē cóng gé
V012-N00026.definitions: The nature of metal is to be adaptable and changeable. The metal element is used to express chilliness, descending, purification, etc.

V012-N00027.label: 长夏
V012-N00027.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五行类
V012-N00027.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00027.definitions: A fifth seasonal period occurring between the seasons of summer and autumn. It can also refer to the period of the last eighteen days of each season.

V012-N00028.label: 五运六气类
V012-N00028.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语
V012-N00028.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00028.englishNames: Five movements and six qi; five circuits and six qi

V012-N00029.label: 主气
V012-N00029.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00029.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00029.englishNames: dominant qi; Host qi; host climatic qi
V012-N00029.definitions: The collective name of wind, cold, dampness, dryness, sovereign fire and ministerial fire, being opposed to the guest qi. It reflects the normal seasonal climatic changes of the year. The calculation is to relate twenty four solar terms to six qi in six steps. The four solar terms consist of one step, being six steps for one year. The calculation starts from the severe cold.

V012-N00030.label: 主运
V012-N00030.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00030.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00030.englishNames: Host movement; domination in circuit by element qi; host evolutive phase; domination in circuit
V012-N00030.definitions: Each of the five movements dominates in spring, summer, later summer, autumn and winter. Starting from the severe cold and following the order of generating cycle of five elements, the first movement is wood, the second is fire, the third is earth, the fourth is metal, and the fifth is water.

V012-N00031.label: 五运
V012-N00031.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00031.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00031.englishNames: five circuits; Five movements; five evolutive phases
V012-N00031.definitions: The collective name of wood movement, fire movement, earth movement, metal movement, and water movement. It reflects the changes of qi of five elements between heaven and earth. Ten heavenly stems of jia, yi, bing, ding, wu, ji, geng, xin, ren, and gui are used to record the movements.

V012-N00032.label: 五运六气
V012-N00032.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00032.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00032.aliases: 运气（中医）； D 5 Elem Evol 6 Natur Factors； Five Circuits and Six Qi； Five Elements' Evolution & Six Kinds of Natural Factors； Five Evolutive Phases and Six Climatic Factors
V012-N00032.englishNames: five circuits and six qi; Five movements and six qi
V012-N00032.pinyinNames: wǔ yùn liù qì; wu yun liu qi
V012-N00032.definitions: A collective name of wood movement, fire movement, earth movement, metal movement, water movement, and the collective name of wind, summer heat (heat), dampness, dryness, fire, cold in the natural world.

V012-N00033.label: 六元
V012-N00033.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00033.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00033.englishNames: Six origins; six essentials; six pathogenic factors; six climatic conditions
V012-N00033.definitions: The combination of wind, cold, dampness, dryness, sovereign fire, ministerial fire with host qi in six steps. The initial qi is in association with Jueyin, wind and wood, the second qi with Shaoyin and sovereign fire, the third qi with Shaoyang and ministerial fire, and the forth qi with Taiyin, dampness and earth, the fifth qi with yang ming, dryness and metal, the sixth (final) qi with Taiyang, cold and water.

V012-N00034.label: 六气
V012-N00034.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00034.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00034.englishNames: six qi; six climatic factors; Six qi
V012-N00034.definitions: The six normal climatic phenomena of wind, summer heat (heat), dampness, dryness, fire, and cold in the natural world. The twelve earthly branches of zi, chou, yin, mou, chen, si, wu, wei, shen, you, xu, and hai are used to record the six qi.

V012-N00035.label: 司天
V012-N00035.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00035.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00035.englishNames: celestial manager qi; celestial control; Celestial controlling
V012-N00035.definitions: The guest qi governing the climatic changes of the first half of the year.

V012-N00036.label: 同天符
V012-N00036.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00036.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00036.englishNames: Identical heavenly complements; same celestial correspondence
V012-N00036.pinyinNames: tóng tiān fú; tong tian fu
V012-N00036.definitions: The heavenly stem and the earthly branch of the year are both yang and the movement of the year and the terrestrial qi share the same category of the five elements in one year.

V012-N00037.label: 同岁会
V012-N00037.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00037.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00037.pinyinNames: tong sui hui; tóng suì huì
V012-N00037.definitions: The heavenly stem and the earthly branch of the year are both yin and the movement of the year and the terrestrial qi share the same category of the five elements in one year.

V012-N00038.label: 君火
V012-N00038.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00038.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00038.definitions: The fire in the heart. It is opposite to the ministerial fire.

V012-N00039.label: 在泉
V012-N00039.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00039.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00039.englishNames: Terrestrial controlling; terrestrial effect; qi in the earth
V012-N00039.definitions: The guest qi governing the climatic changes of the second half of the year. It is opposite to celestial qi.

V012-N00040.label: 地支
V012-N00040.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00040.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00040.definitions: The collective name of zi, chou, yin, mou, chen, si, wu, wei, shen, you, xu, and hai.

V012-N00041.label: 复气
V012-N00041.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00041.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00041.definitions: The qi that is weaker and is restrained from another qi in the five movements and six qi.

V012-N00042.label: 天干
V012-N00042.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00042.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00042.definitions: The collective name of jia, yi, bing, ding, wu, ji, geng, xin, ren, and gui.

V012-N00043.label: 天符
V012-N00043.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00043.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00043.englishNames: coincidence of heavenly qi; Heavenly complements; celestial correspondence
V012-N00043.definitions: The movement qi of the year and the celestial qi share the same category of the five elements in one year.

V012-N00044.label: 太乙天符
V012-N00044.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00044.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00044.englishNames: celestial correspondence in convergent year; Taiyi heavenly complements
V012-N00044.pinyinNames: tài yǐ tiān fú; tai yi tian fu
V012-N00044.definitions: The movement of the year, the celestial qi, and earthly branch of the year share the same category of the five elements in one year.

V012-N00045.label: 客气
V012-N00045.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00045.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00045.englishNames: guest climatic qi; subordinate qi; guest qi; exogenous pathogenic qi; Guest qi
V012-N00045.definitions: 1) Reflects the abnormal climatic changes of the year, being opposed to the host qi. It includes the celestial qi, the terrestrial qi, and the four right and left intervening qi. The first yin is in association with Jueyin, wind, and wood, the second yin with Shaoyin and sovereign fire, and the third yin with Taiyin, dampness, and earth. The first yang is in association with Shaoyang and ministerial fire, the second yang with yang ming, dryness, and metal, the third yang with Taiyang, cold, and water. The calculation is to combine the above yin–yang order with twelve earthly branches. 2) External pathogenic factors.

V012-N00046.label: 客运
V012-N00046.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00046.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00046.englishNames: gust circuit; subordination of circuit; guest evolutive phase; Guest movement
V012-N00046.definitions: The special climatic changes of the five seasons. It varies in every season in every year. Starting from the movement of the year as the initial movement, calculate in five steps according to the excess and insufficiency order of five elements. Ten years is a complete cycle and covers ten heavenly stems. Ten years is a complete cycle that covers ten heavenly stems. Then the cycle starts all over agin.

V012-N00047.label: 岁会
V012-N00047.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00047.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00047.englishNames: convergent year; yearly weather; Annual congruence
V012-N00047.definitions: The movement qi of the year and the earthly branch of the year share the same category of the five elements in one year.

V012-N00048.label: 岁运
V012-N00048.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00048.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00048.englishNames: circuit of year; year evolutive phase; Movement of the year
V012-N00048.definitions: The movement governs the year, being jia and ji in category of earth movement, yi and geng in category of metal movement, bing and xin in category of water movement, ding and ren in category of wood movement, and wu and gui in category of fire movement. For example, jia and ji is in category of earth movement, the movement of the year of the jia-zi year is earth.

V012-N00049.label: 平气
V012-N00049.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00049.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00049.englishNames: normal circuit qi; normal climatic factors; Balanced movements
V012-N00049.definitions: The excess of qi is restrained and the insufficiency of qi is compensated in the five movements.

V012-N00050.label: 本气
V012-N00050.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00050.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00050.definitions: Six qi of heaven, being wind, heat, dampness, dryness, cold and fire.

V012-N00051.label: 标本中气
V012-N00051.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00051.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00051.englishNames: manifestation、root cause and medial qi; manifestation， root cause and medial qi; Tip qi, root qi and middle qi; human-heavenly-medial qi
V012-N00051.pinyinNames: biao ben zhong qi; biāo běn zhōng qì
V012-N00051.definitions: The study of relationship between six qi of heaven and six qi of the human body. It is used to determine the principle of the treatment based on interactions between human and nature.

V012-N00052.label: 标气
V012-N00052.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00052.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00052.definitions: Qi of six meridians of Shaoyang, Taiyang, Yangming, Shaoyin, Taiyin and Jueyin.

V012-N00053.label: 甲子
V012-N00053.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00053.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00053.definitions: The heavenly stems pair with the earthly branches, following their orders, from jia–zi to gui–hai, forming a complete cycle of sixty years. The heavenly stems initiate with jia and the earthly branches initiate with zi. The combination is used to number years, months, days and times.

V012-N00054.label: 相火
V012-N00054.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00054.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00054.definitions: The fire dwelling in the liver, gallbladder, kidney and triple energizer, being opposite to the sovereign fire.

V012-N00055.label: 胜复
V012-N00055.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00055.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00055.englishNames: Prevailing and retaliation; dominantion and retaliation
V012-N00055.definitions: The relationship between the prevailing qi and the retaliating qi in five movements and six qi. They coexist by increasing and decreasing in the same direction.

V012-N00056.label: 胜气
V012-N00056.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00056.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00056.definitions: The qi that is stronger and restrain another qi in the five movements and six qi.

V012-N00057.label: 迁正
V012-N00057.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00057.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00057.definitions: The intervening qi left to the celestial qi becomes the celestial qi and the intervening qi left to the terrestrial qi becomes the terrestrial qi.

V012-N00058.label: 运气同化
V012-N00058.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00058.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00058.englishNames: assimilation of circuit and qi; Compatibility of movements and qi
V012-N00058.pinyinNames: yun qi tong hua; yùn qì tóng huà
V012-N00058.definitions: The five movements and six qi share the same category of the five elements.

V012-N00059.label: 退位
V012-N00059.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00059.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00059.definitions: The celestial qi becomes the intervening qi right to the celestial qi and the terrestrial qi becomes the intervening qi right to the terrestrial qi.

V012-N00060.label: 间气
V012-N00060.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜五运六气类
V012-N00060.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00060.englishNames: qi at sides; intermediate qi; Intervening qi
V012-N00060.definitions: The qi left and right to the celestial qi and the qi left and right to the terrestrial qi.

V012-N00061.label: 其他类
V012-N00061.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语
V012-N00061.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00062.label: 七冲门
V012-N00062.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00062.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00062.definitions: Seven major places in the digestive tract, being flying gate, door gate, breathing gate, rushing gate, pylorus, screen gate and corporeal gate.

V012-N00063.label: 中医基础理论
V012-N00063.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00063.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00063.aliases: Basic Theories (TCM)； Basic theory of traditional Chinese medicine
V012-N00063.englishNames: Basic Theory of Traditional Chinese Medicine; Fundamentals of traditional Chinese medicine; fundamental theory of traditional Chinese medicine
V012-N00063.pinyinNames: zhong yi ji chu li lun; zhōng yī jī chǔ lǐ lùn
V012-N00063.definitions: Rational understandings of fundamental knowledge in traditional Chinese medicine. These include essential concepts, regular patterns, theories and principles. These understandings guide the application of traditional Chinese medicine in health preservation and disease prevention, diagnosis and treatment as well as rehabilitation.

V012-N00064.label: 中医学/中医
V012-N00064.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00064.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00064.definitions: A comprehensive system originated in ancient China that involves the study of life, health and diseases.

V012-N00065.label: 五轮
V012-N00065.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00065.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00065.definitions: A collective name of blood wheel, wind wheel, flesh wheel, qi wheel and water wheel for the eye, which is closely related to the physiological conditions or/and pathological changes.

V012-N00066.label: 传统医学
V012-N00066.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00066.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00066.definitions: The sum total of the knowledge, skills and practices based on the theories, beliefs and experiences indigenous to different cultures, whether explicable or not, used in the maintenance of health as well as in the prevention, diagnosis, improvement or treatment of physical and mental illness.

V012-N00067.label: 八廓
V012-N00067.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00067.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00067.definitions: The eyes are categorized into eight regions according to zang–fu theory to match with the eight trigrams. This helps to further categorize and differentiate patterns of eye disease.

V012-N00068.label: 前阴
V012-N00068.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00068.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00069.label: 后阴
V012-N00069.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00069.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00070.label: 吸门
V012-N00070.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00070.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00071.label: 唇四白
V012-N00071.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00071.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00071.definitions: The area around the lips reflecting the strength and the weakness of the essential qi in the spleen.

V012-N00072.label: 四末
V012-N00072.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00072.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00072.definitions: 1) Four limbs. 2) Distal ends of toes and fingers.

V012-N00073.label: 天人合一
V012-N00073.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00073.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00073.definitions: The oneness of human beings, heaven, earth and nature.

V012-N00074.label: 天人相应
V012-N00074.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00074.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00074.aliases: 天人合一； Man Universe Correspondence
V012-N00074.englishNames: correspondence between nature and human; correspondence between human body and natural environment; Man–nature correspondence; Correspondence between Human Body and Natural Environment
V012-N00074.pinyinNames: tian ren xiang ying; tiān rén xiāng yìng
V012-N00074.definitions: Man and nature correspond to each other.

V012-N00075.label: 宗筋
V012-N00075.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00075.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00075.englishNames: Gathering of muscle around genital region
V012-N00075.definitions: 1) Muscle regions of three yang and three yin meridians gathering at the genital regions. 2) Male external genitalia.

V012-N00076.label: 官窍
V012-N00076.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00076.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00076.definitions: There are five sense organs, namely, the tongue, nose, mouth, eyes and ears. The throat is also considered as a sense organ. There are a total of nine orifices (seven yang orifices and two yin orifices), namely, the eyes (2), ears (2), nostrils (2), mouth, anterior yin (external genitalia including the external orifice of the urethra) and posterior yin (anus).

V012-N00077.label: 幽门
V012-N00077.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00077.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00078.label: 户门
V012-N00078.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00078.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00079.label: 整体观念
V012-N00079.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00079.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00079.englishNames: Holistic concept; concept of holism; holism
V012-N00079.pinyinNames: zhěng tǐ guān niàn; zheng ti guan nian
V012-N00079.definitions: The integral entity of the human body. The integration of human beings with nature and society.

V012-N00080.label: 明堂
V012-N00080.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00080.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00080.definitions: An ancient term for the nose. It also refers to the chart of the twelve meridians and points in Chinese medicine.

V012-N00081.label: 气轮
V012-N00081.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00081.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00081.definitions: Refers to the white part of the eye, pertaining to the lung. The lung governs qi. Its disorders are related to the lung.

V012-N00082.label: 气门
V012-N00082.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00082.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00082.definitions: Another name of the sweat pore for inflow and outflow of qi.

V012-N00083.label: 水轮
V012-N00083.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00083.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00083.definitions: Refers to the pupil, pertaining to the kidney. The kidney governs water. Its disorders are related to the kidney.

V012-N00084.label: 溪谷
V012-N00084.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00084.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00084.definitions: The empty space or depression at the connection of muscles. The large one known as the “valley” and the small one as the “creek”.

V012-N00085.label: 玄府
V012-N00085.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00085.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00085.definitions: Another name of the sweat pore for dispersion and flowing of qi and fluid.

V012-N00086.label: 白睛
V012-N00086.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00086.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00086.definitions: The white opaque part of the outer surface of the eyeball, i.e. the conjunctiva and sclera.

V012-N00087.label: 瞳神
V012-N00087.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00087.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00087.definitions: In a narrow sense, it refers to the opening at the centre of the iris, posterior to the cornea, through which light enters the eye. In a broad sense, it refers to the pupil and all attached tissues. Since this part corresponds to the kidney (the water wheel according to the five-wheel theory) and the liver and kidney share the same source, pupil-related eye problems are often associated with the liver and kidney.

V012-N00088.label: 神
V012-N00088.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00088.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00088.definitions: 1) Dominance of life activities. 2) Outer manifestation reflecting the life activities. 3) State of mind, mood and mental activities.

V012-N00089.label: 神明
V012-N00089.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00089.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00089.definitions: Mind or spirit. Alternatively, it also refers to all natural phenomena and patterns, including the sun, moon and stars.

V012-N00090.label: 神水
V012-N00090.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00090.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00090.definitions: Tears and transparent, watery fluid secreted from the ciliary epithelium.

V012-N00091.label: 神膏
V012-N00091.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00091.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00091.definitions: The clear, colourless gel that fills the space between the lens and the retina of the eyeball.

V012-N00092.label: 秽浊
V012-N00092.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00092.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00092.definitions: 1) An exogenous pathogenic factor. 2) Special odour of the patients’ body, excretion and secretion. 3) Pathological products steamed by damp heat.

V012-N00093.label: 精室
V012-N00093.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00093.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00093.definitions: The place where the male stores the essence, including testicles, epididymis, seminal vesicles and prostate. It is associated with reproduction.

V012-N00094.label: 肉轮
V012-N00094.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00094.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00094.definitions: Refers to the eyelids, pertaining to the spleen. The spleen governs flesh. Its disorders are related to the spleen.

V012-N00095.label: 腠理
V012-N00095.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00095.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00095.definitions: Refers to the striae of the skin, muscle and organs, also to the space between the skin, muscle and organs, the minute passages for the fluid percolation and the flowing of qi and blood.

V012-N00096.label: 膏肓
V012-N00096.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00096.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00096.definitions: The space inferior to the heart and superior to the diaphragm. When used as a disease location, it often means that the condition is incurable.

V012-N00097.label: 膻中
V012-N00097.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00097.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00097.definitions: 1) The gathering venue of pectoral qi, at the centre of the two nipples. 2) Another name of pericardium. 3) Name of an acupuncture point located in the midpoint between two nipples.

V012-N00098.label: 虚里
V012-N00098.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00098.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00098.definitions: The major collateral of the stomach, one of the sixteen collaterals, located at the apex of heart below the left nipple, which is the gathering venue of pectoral qi.

V012-N00099.label: 血室
V012-N00099.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00099.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00099.definitions: A term used to refer to: 1) uterus; 2) Chong meridian; or 3) liver.

V012-N00100.label: 血轮
V012-N00100.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00100.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00100.definitions: Refers to blood vessels at the canthus of eyes, pertaining to the heart. The heart governs blood. Its disorders are related to the heart.

V012-N00101.label: 贲门
V012-N00101.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00101.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00102.label: 阑门
V012-N00102.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00102.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00102.definitions: Junction between large and small intestines.

V012-N00103.label: 风轮
V012-N00103.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00103.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00103.definitions: Refers to the black part of the eye, pertaining to the liver. The liver is the organ of wind and wood. Its disorders are related to the liver.

V012-N00104.label: 飞门
V012-N00104.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00104.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00105.label: 魄门
V012-N00105.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00105.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00106.label: 黑睛
V012-N00106.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜其他类
V012-N00106.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00106.definitions: The transparent membranous structure forming the central anterior part of the eye, i.e. the cornea. Since this part corresponds to the liver, corneal eye problems are often associated with the liver and gallbladder.

V012-N00107.label: 养生预防类
V012-N00107.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语
V012-N00107.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00108.label: 养生
V012-N00108.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜养生预防类
V012-N00108.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00108.definitions: The activities to maintain physical and mental health and prevent illnesses.

V012-N00109.label: 四气调神
V012-N00109.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜养生预防类
V012-N00109.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00109.englishNames: Regulating spirit according to four seasonal qi
V012-N00109.definitions: The adjustment of mental activities along with the climate of four seasons.

V012-N00110.label: 天年
V012-N00110.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜养生预防类
V012-N00110.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00110.englishNames: Natural life span; natural life span

V012-N00111.label: 形与神俱
V012-N00111.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜养生预防类
V012-N00111.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00111.englishNames: harmony of body and spirit; Healthy body and mind
V012-N00111.pinyinNames: xin yu shen ju; xíng yǔ shén jù
V012-N00111.definitions: The physical appearance and the mental activities are inseparable. The robust physical appearance and sound vitality is the sign of body–mind wellness.

V012-N00112.label: 既病防变
V012-N00112.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜养生预防类
V012-N00112.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00112.englishNames: Preventing transmission of a disease after its onset
V012-N00112.definitions: A method to use early diagnosis and early intervention to prevent disease progression and transmission.

V012-N00113.label: 春夏养阳
V012-N00113.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜养生预防类
V012-N00113.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00113.englishNames: Protecting yang in spring and summer
V012-N00113.definitions: Yang qi rises and grows in the spring and the summer. It is necessary to protect and nourish the internal yang qi to keep it sufficient along with the seasons.

V012-N00114.label: 未病先防
V012-N00114.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜养生预防类
V012-N00114.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00114.englishNames: Preventing a disease before it arises
V012-N00114.definitions: A method to strengthen the healthy qi and eliminate potential harmful factors to prevent the occurrence of a disease.

V012-N00115.label: 治未病
V012-N00115.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜养生预防类
V012-N00115.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00115.englishNames: Preventing a disease before it arises
V012-N00115.definitions: A concept to prevent a disease before it arises and prevents transmission of a disease after its onset.

V012-N00116.label: 秋冬养阴
V012-N00116.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜养生预防类
V012-N00116.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00116.englishNames: Nourishing yin in autumn and winter
V012-N00116.definitions: Everything is stored in the autumn and the winter. It is necessary to protect and store the internal yin essence to keep it internally accumulated along with the seasons.

V012-N00117.label: 病因类
V012-N00117.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语
V012-N00117.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00117.englishNames: Cause of Disease; disease cause; Etiological factors

V012-N00118.label: 七伤
V012-N00118.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00118.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00118.englishNames: seven damages; Seven damages; seven injuries
V012-N00118.definitions: Refers to the seven pathogenic factors that lead to deficiency and consumption, including improper diet, anxiety, drink, sex, hunger, over-exertion, and damage to meridians, collaterals, Ying-nutrients, Wei-defense and qi.

V012-N00119.label: 七情
V012-N00119.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00119.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00119.englishNames: seven emotions; Seven emotions; seven relations of medicinal compatibility
V012-N00119.definitions: Seven normal emotions include joy, anger, anxiety, overthinking, grief, fear and fright.

V012-N00120.label: 七情内伤
V012-N00120.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00120.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00120.definitions: A collective term for damage caused by extreme emotions including joy, anger, anxiety, overthinking, grief, fear and fright.

V012-N00121.label: 三因学说
V012-N00121.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00121.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00121.englishNames: Theory of three etiological categories; theory of three categories of disease cause; theory of three types of disease causes; theroy of three types of disease cause
V012-N00121.pinyinNames: sān yīn xué shuō; san yin xue shuo
V012-N00121.definitions: A theory that classifies etiological factors into internal, external and neither internal nor external.

V012-N00122.label: 不内外因
V012-N00122.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00122.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00122.englishNames: Neither internal nor external etiological factors; cause neither internal nor external; non-endo-non-exogenous cause; non-endo-exogenous
V012-N00122.pinyinNames: bù nèi wài yīn; bu nei wai yin
V012-N00122.definitions: Pathogenic factors that are not categorized as external or internal, such as improper diet, over-exertion, traumatic injuries, animal/insect bites, drowning etc.

V012-N00123.label: 五劳
V012-N00123.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00123.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00123.englishNames: Five exhaustions; five kinds of overstrain; five consumptions; five kinds of strain; five kinds of consuptive diseases
V012-N00123.definitions: The five exhaustions are listed as follows: Long-time observation damages blood; long-time lying damages qi; long-time sitting damages muscles; long-time standing damages bones; and long-time walking damages sinews. Alternatively, the five overstrains refer to deficiencies of the five zang organs (i.e. liver, heart, spleen, lung and kidney).

V012-N00124.label: 五味偏嗜
V012-N00124.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00124.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00124.englishNames: five-flavor preference; Flavour preference; flavor predilection
V012-N00124.pinyinNames: wǔ wèi piān shì; wu wei pian shi
V012-N00124.definitions: The five flavours are spicy, sweet, sour, bitter and salty. Habitual preference for a particular flavour or taste may bring about adverse reactions or damage the five zang organs.

V012-N00125.label: 五志化火
V012-N00125.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00125.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00125.englishNames: transformation of the five minds into fire; Five emotions transforming into fire; five emotions transforming into fire; five minds transforming into fire
V012-N00125.pinyinNames: wǔ zhì huà huǒ; wu zhi hua huo
V012-N00125.definitions: Fire pattern transformed from disturbance of joy, anger, grief, overthinking and fear.

V012-N00126.label: 伏饮
V012-N00126.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00126.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00126.definitions: A type of fluid retention that is deeply located and hard to remove.

V012-N00127.label: 六淫
V012-N00127.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00127.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00127.englishNames: Six pathogenic factors; six climatic exopathogens; six climatic influences; Six Climatic Exopathogens; six excesses
V012-N00127.definitions: A collective term for six exogenous pathogenic factors: wind, cold, summer-heat, dampness, dryness and fire.

V012-N00128.label: 六郁
V012-N00128.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00128.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00128.definitions: A collective term for stagnation of qi, blood, phlegm, fire, food and dampness. Often results from qi stagnation of the liver and spleen.

V012-N00129.label: 内伤病因
V012-N00129.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00129.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00129.englishNames: cause of endogenous injury; Endogenous etiological factors
V012-N00129.definitions: Internal dysfunctions due to extreme emotions, over-exertion, and improper diet.

V012-N00130.label: 内寒
V012-N00130.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00130.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00130.englishNames: internal cold; Internal cold; endogenous cold
V012-N00130.definitions: Opposite to external cold, it is a pathological state of deficiency cold due to yang qi deficiency of the zang–fu organs.

V012-N00131.label: 内所因
V012-N00131.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00131.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00131.definitions: These mainly refer to seven emotional disturbances.

V012-N00132.label: 内湿
V012-N00132.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00132.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00132.englishNames: endogenous dampness; Internal dampness; internal dampness
V012-N00132.definitions: Opposite to external dampness, it is a pathological state of retained water, dampness, phlegm and turbidity due to dysfunctions of the zang–fu organs and disordered water metabolism.

V012-N00133.label: 内火
V012-N00133.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00133.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00133.definitions: Opposite to external fire, it is a pathological state of fire heat due to disordered qi, blood, yin and yang of the zang–fu organs.

V012-N00134.label: 内燥
V012-N00134.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00134.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00134.englishNames: internal dryness; endogenous dryness; Internal dryness
V012-N00134.definitions: Opposite to external dryness, it is a pathological state of body fluids failing to moisten the body.

V012-N00135.label: 内生五邪
V012-N00135.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00135.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00135.definitions: The five pathological states of wind, fire, cold, dryness and dampness due to dysfunctions of the zang–fu organs.

V012-N00136.label: 内风
V012-N00136.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00136.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00136.aliases: 肝风内动； 虚风内动； Endogenous wind
V012-N00136.englishNames: internal wind; endogenous wind; Internal wind
V012-N00136.definitions: Opposite to external wind, it is a pathological state of yang qi hyperactivity and wind stirring due to disordered qi and blood of the zang–fu organs. This includes extreme heat generating wind, liver yang transforming into wind, blood deficiency generating wind and yin deficiency stirring wind.

V012-N00137.label: 劳力过度
V012-N00137.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00137.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00137.definitions: Excessive physical activities damage the zang–fu organs and cause qi deficiency of the zang organs.

V012-N00138.label: 劳神过度
V012-N00138.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00138.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00138.definitions: Excessive mental activities tend to damage heart blood and spleen qi.

V012-N00139.label: 劳逸失度
V012-N00139.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00139.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00139.englishNames: maladjustment between work and rest; Work–rest imbalance
V012-N00139.definitions: A collective term for over-exertion and physical inactivity.

V012-N00140.label: 医过
V012-N00140.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00140.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00140.definitions: A pathogenic factor that induces or aggravates medical conditions by errors of health care professionals.

V012-N00141.label: 喜伤心
V012-N00141.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00141.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00141.englishNames: over-joy damaging heart; Excessive joy damages the heart
V012-N00141.pinyinNames: xi shang xin; xǐ shāng xīn
V012-N00141.definitions: Excessive joy damages heart spirit, consumes heart qi and results in mental derangement.

V012-N00142.label: 壮火
V012-N00142.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00142.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00142.definitions: Hyperactive, pathogenic fire that consumes healthy qi.

V012-N00143.label: 外感病因
V012-N00143.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00143.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00143.englishNames: Exogenous etiological factors; cause of exogenous contraction
V012-N00143.definitions: Pathogenic factors that attack the body through the skin, mouth and nose, including six pathogenic factors and epidemic qi.

V012-N00144.label: 外所因
V012-N00144.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00144.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00144.definitions: These mainly refer to six exogenous pathogenic factors.

V012-N00145.label: 实邪
V012-N00145.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00145.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00145.englishNames: excess pathogen; Excess pathogens; pathogen from child-organ; excessive pathogen
V012-N00145.definitions: Opposite to deficiency pathogens, it is a collective term for pathogenic factors. Alternatively, it refers to pathogenic qi that transmits from the son organ to the mother organ.

V012-N00146.label: 客邪
V012-N00146.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00146.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00146.englishNames: intruding pathogen; visiting pathogen; Exogenous pathogenic factors
V012-N00146.definitions: External pathogenic factors that damage the human body.

V012-N00147.label: 寒性凝滞
V012-N00147.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00147.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00147.englishNames: cold having property of coagulation and stagnation; Cold congeals and stagnates; cold attributing to congealing
V012-N00147.definitions: Pathogenic cold causes qi/blood to stagnate, blocks meridians, and causes pain.

V012-N00148.label: 寒性收引
V012-N00148.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00148.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00148.englishNames: cold having property of contraction; cold attributing to contraction; Cold causes contraction/contracture
V012-N00148.definitions: Pathogenic cold constricts qi, blocks the skin pores, and causes contractions.

V012-N00149.label: 寒易伤阳
V012-N00149.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00149.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00149.englishNames: cold tending to injure yang; cold pathogen being apt to attack yang; Cold tends to impair yang
V012-N00149.definitions: Cold is yin in nature and can easily impair yang qi.

V012-N00150.label: 寒邪
V012-N00150.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00150.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00150.englishNames: Pathogenic cold; Pathogenic Cold; cold pathogen
V012-N00150.definitions: A pathogenic factor characterized by coldness, stagnation, congealing and contraction.

V012-N00151.label: 怒伤肝
V012-N00151.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00151.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00151.englishNames: anger damaging liver; Excessive anger damages the liver
V012-N00151.pinyinNames: nu shang gan; nù shāng gān
V012-N00151.definitions: Excessive anger damages liver qi, impairs the function of liver in maintaining the free flow of qi, causes the qi to rise, and results in disordered qi and blood.

V012-N00152.label: 思伤脾
V012-N00152.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00152.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00152.englishNames: Excessive overthinking damages the spleen; thought damaging spleen
V012-N00152.pinyinNames: sī shāng pí; si shang pi
V012-N00152.definitions: Excessive overthinking damages spleen qi, causes qi to stagnate, and impairs the function of the spleen in transportation and transformation.

V012-N00153.label: 恐伤肾
V012-N00153.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00153.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00153.englishNames: fear damaging kidney; Excessive fear damages the kidney
V012-N00153.pinyinNames: kong shang shen; kǒng shāng shèn
V012-N00153.definitions: Excessive fear damages kidney qi, causes kidney qi to descend, and impairs the function of the kidney in storing essence.

V012-N00154.label: 悲伤肺
V012-N00154.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00154.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00154.definitions: Excessive grief damages and consumes lung qi and impairs the dispersal of lung qi.

V012-N00155.label: 房劳过度
V012-N00155.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00155.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00155.definitions: Excessive sexual activities consume and damage kidney essence.

V012-N00156.label: 无形之痰
V012-N00156.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00156.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00156.definitions: Intangible phlegm is invisible and impalpable; however, it can cause pathogenic features of phlegm.

V012-N00157.label: 时邪
V012-N00157.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00157.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00157.definitions: A collective term for epidemic, etiological factors associated with the four seasons.

V012-N00158.label: 暑多夹湿
V012-N00158.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00158.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00158.englishNames: Summer heat often combines with dampness
V012-N00158.definitions: Summer is hot, humid and rainy. The summer heat provokes dampness and is often liable to mix itself with dampness.

V012-N00159.label: 暑性升散
V012-N00159.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00159.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00159.englishNames: summerheat attributing to rise and dispersion; Summer heat ascends and disperses; characteristic of summerheat being ascending and dispersive
V012-N00159.definitions: Summer heat ascends and disperses. Often directly affects the qi phase, opens the sweating pores and damages body fluids.

V012-N00160.label: 暑性炎热
V012-N00160.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00160.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00160.englishNames: summer attributing to burning heat; characteristic of summerheat being scorchinghot; Summer heat causes flaming heat
V012-N00160.definitions: Pathogenic summer heat is characterized by extreme hotness. Often causes hyperactive yang heat.

V012-N00161.label: 暑邪
V012-N00161.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00161.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00161.englishNames: summerheat pathogen; Pathogenic summer heat; Pathogenic Summer Heat
V012-N00161.definitions: A pathogenic factor commonly seen between the summer solstice and beginning of autumn. It is characterized by flames ascending and dispersing.

V012-N00162.label: 有形之痰
V012-N00162.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00162.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00162.definitions: Tangible phlegm is visible, touchable and audible when it is secreted from the airways.

V012-N00163.label: 毒邪
V012-N00163.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00163.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00163.definitions: Toxic pathogens include toxins transformed from six exogenous pathogenic factors, toxic fire from sores/ulcers, toxic heat, snake venom, rabies or toxic lacquer.

V012-N00164.label: 温邪
V012-N00164.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00164.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00164.definitions: A collective term for pathogenic warm heat, including spring warmth, wind warmth, summer heat warmth, damp warmth, autumn dryness, winter warmth, pestilence warmth, warm toxin, warm malaria, etc.

V012-N00165.label: 湿性趋下
V012-N00165.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00165.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00165.englishNames: dampness characterized by downward going; Dampness tends to descend; characteristic of dampness being descending
V012-N00165.definitions: Dampness move downward and affects the yin and lower parts of the body.

V012-N00166.label: 湿性重浊
V012-N00166.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00166.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00166.englishNames: characteristic of dampness being heavy and turbid; Dampness is heavy and turbid; dampness attributing to heaciness and turbidity
V012-N00166.definitions: Pathogenic dampness is characterized by its heaviness and turbidity. The diseases caused by it may lead to heaviness of the body and turbidity of secretions and excrements.

V012-N00167.label: 湿性黏滞
V012-N00167.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00167.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00167.englishNames: Dampness is sticky and stagnant; dampness attributing to viscosity and stagnation; characteristic of dampness being sticky and stagnant
V012-N00167.definitions: Dampness is characterized by stickiness and stagnation. It causes sticky, viscous secretions and discharges. Dampness-induced conditions often last a long time.

V012-N00168.label: 湿胜阳微
V012-N00168.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00168.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00168.englishNames: predominant dampness weakening yang; predominant dampness causing weak yang; When damp prevails, yang declines
V012-N00168.pinyinNames: shī shèng yáng wēi; shi sheng yang wei
V012-N00168.definitions: Dampness is yin in nature and can easily damage yang qi. It is tangible and can obstruct the flow of yang qi.

V012-N00169.label: 湿邪
V012-N00169.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00169.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00169.englishNames: Pathogenic dampness; dampness pathogen; Pathogenic Dampness
V012-N00169.definitions: A pathogenic factor that obstructs the flow of qi. It is characterized by heaviness, turbidity, stickiness and downward flowing.

V012-N00170.label: 湿阻气机
V012-N00170.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00170.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00170.englishNames: Dampness obstructs qi movement; dampness hampering qi movement
V012-N00170.definitions: Dampness is a tangible pathogenic factor and is easy to obstruct qi movement.

V012-N00171.label: 火性炎上
V012-N00171.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00171.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00171.englishNames: characteristic of fire being flaring up; fire characterized by flaming; Fire flames upward
V012-N00171.definitions: The nature of pathogenic fire flames upward. It causes heat symptoms and often affects the upper part of the body.

V012-N00172.label: 火易动血
V012-N00172.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00172.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00172.englishNames: fire tending to cause bleeding; Fire tends to cause bleeding
V012-N00172.definitions: Since fire may burn the vessels and collaterals and cause the blood to move recklessly, it often results in bleeding or macular eruption.

V012-N00173.label: 火易扰心神
V012-N00173.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00173.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00173.aliases: Fire tends to disturb the heart Shen
V012-N00173.englishNames: Fire tends to disturb the heart spirit
V012-N00173.definitions: Since fire tends to disturb the heart spirit, it often causes mental restlessness.

V012-N00174.label: 火易生疮疡
V012-N00174.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00174.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00174.definitions: Since fire heat tends to enter the blood and remain in a fixed location, it often causes sores/ulcers.

V012-N00175.label: 火易生风
V012-N00175.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00175.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00175.englishNames: fire tending to cause wind; Fire tends to generate wind
V012-N00175.definitions: Fire tends to stir liver wind. Extreme heat can cause stirring of wind.

V012-N00176.label: 火耗气伤津
V012-N00176.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00176.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00176.englishNames: Fire consumes qi and body fluids
V012-N00176.definitions: Since fire consumes healthy qi and scorches yin fluids, it often causes deficiency of both qi and thin fluids.

V012-N00177.label: 火邪
V012-N00177.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00177.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00177.englishNames: fire pathogen; Pathogenic fire; Pathogenic Fire
V012-N00177.definitions: A pathogenic factor that flames upward, consumes qi and body fluids, engenders wind, stirs blood, and harasses the heart spirit.

V012-N00178.label: 热邪
V012-N00178.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00178.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00178.aliases: 温邪； Heat Pathogen； 火热
V012-N00178.englishNames: heat pathogen; Pathogenic Heat; Pathogenic heat
V012-N00178.definitions: A pathogenic factor that causes excessive pathological changes of hot and yang nature.

V012-N00179.label: 燥性干涩
V012-N00179.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00179.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00179.englishNames: dryness being dry and puckery; Dryness tends to desiccate; dryness characterized by aridity and astringency
V012-N00179.pinyinNames: zao xing gan se; zào xìng gān sè
V012-N00179.definitions: Dryness desiccates by consuming body fluids and causing body fluid deficiency and lack of moisture.

V012-N00180.label: 燥易伤肺
V012-N00180.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00180.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00180.englishNames: dryness tending to injure lung; dryness likely to injure lung; Dryness easily damages the lung
V012-N00180.definitions: Dryness invades the body through the nose, mouth and skin pores. It easily damages the lung fluids and impairs the dispersing of lung qi.

V012-N00181.label: 燥邪
V012-N00181.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00181.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00181.englishNames: Pathogenic Dryness; dryness pathogen; Pathogenic dryness
V012-N00181.definitions: A pathogenic factor that consumes body fluids and tends to damage the lung.

V012-N00182.label: 生痰之源
V012-N00182.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00182.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00182.definitions: The spleen governs transportation and transformation of water and food. The failure of the spleen to transport and transform water fluids may cause internal retention of water dampness and generate phlegm.

V012-N00183.label: 留饮
V012-N00183.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00183.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00183.definitions: Fluid retention that lasts a long period of time. It is one type of fluid retention.

V012-N00184.label: 疠气
V012-N00184.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00184.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00184.englishNames: Pestilence qi; pestilent qi; pestilential qi
V012-N00184.definitions: A collective term for exogenous pathogenic factors with high contagiousness.

V012-N00185.label: 病因
V012-N00185.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00185.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00185.englishNames: Causative factors; cause of disease; disease cause; pathogenic factors

V012-N00186.label: 痰
V012-N00186.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00186.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00186.definitions: Associated with fluid retention. It refers to the viscous, turbid pathological products that accumulate in the body due to disordered water metabolism. It can be an etiological factor for a variety of diseases.

V012-N00187.label: 瘀血
V012-N00187.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00187.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00187.aliases: Stagnant blood； 败血； 恶血； 衃血； 污血； 蓄血
V012-N00187.englishNames: static blood; Static blood; Stagnated Blood; stagnated blood
V012-N00187.definitions: A pathological product formed during slow blood flow or as blood coagulates. It is also a secondary pathogenic factor.

V012-N00188.label: 瘟邪
V012-N00188.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00188.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00188.definitions: A pathogenic factor with high contagiousness.

V012-N00189.label: 禀赋不足
V012-N00189.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00189.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00189.englishNames: constitutional insufficiency; insufficiency of natural endowment; inadequate natural endowment; Constitutional insufficiency
V012-N00189.pinyinNames: bǐng fù bù zú; bing fu bu zu
V012-N00189.definitions: Congenital constitutional weakness is an internal etiological factor of healthy qi deficiency.

V012-N00190.label: 结石
V012-N00190.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00190.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00190.definitions: The gravel-like substance that accumulates in the human body, especially in the ducts and cavities of zang–fu organs. It is also a secondary pathogenic factor.

V012-N00191.label: 胎传
V012-N00191.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00191.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00191.englishNames: fetal infection; Maternal–to–fetal transmission; congenital transmission
V012-N00191.definitions: An inherited etiological factor that transmits the body constitution and disease of the mother to her fetus.

V012-N00192.label: 胎弱
V012-N00192.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00192.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00192.definitions: A collective term for a congenitally weak constitution and a deficiency of qi and blood. It is a major contributing factor to a deficiency of healthy qi.

V012-N00193.label: 胎毒
V012-N00193.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00193.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00193.englishNames: hazards to fetal health; fetal toxin; fetal toxicosis; fetal toxicity
V012-N00193.definitions: A pre-birth toxin. It is a pathogenic factor that causes infantile skin rashes.

V012-N00194.label: 虚邪
V012-N00194.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00194.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00194.englishNames: Deficiency pathogens; deficiency-type pathogen; pathogen from mother-organ; deficient pathogen; pathogen attack in deficiency condition
V012-N00194.definitions: Opposite to excess pathogens, it is a collective term for exogenous pathogenic factors that invade the body when the body is weak. Alternatively, it refers to pathogenic qi that transmits from the mother organ to the son organ.

V012-N00195.label: 虫兽伤
V012-N00195.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00195.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00195.englishNames: injury by animal and insect; bitten by animal and insect; Animal/insect bites
V012-N00195.pinyinNames: chong shou shang; chóng shòu shāng
V012-N00195.definitions: Harm to the body by animals or insects.

V012-N00196.label: 贮痰之器
V012-N00196.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00196.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00196.definitions: The lung dominates water and regulates the water passage. The failure of the lung to disperse qi may cause internal retention of phlegm fluids.

V012-N00197.label: 辨证求因
V012-N00197.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00197.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00197.englishNames: To differentiate pattern to reveal an etiology; syndrome differentiation/pattern identification to determine disease cause
V012-N00197.pinyinNames: biàn zhèng qiú yīn; bian zheng qiu yin
V012-N00197.definitions: A method to seek the etiological factors according to clinical manifestations.

V012-N00198.label: 过劳
V012-N00198.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00198.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00198.definitions: Overwork can be physical, mental or sexual.

V012-N00199.label: 过逸
V012-N00199.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00199.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
