V012-N00199.definitions: Physical inactivity leads to imbalances in the flow of qi and blood, weak tendons and bones, stagnant qi of the spleen and stomach. In severe cases, it may even cause other secondary diseases.

V012-N00200.label: 邪气
V012-N00200.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00200.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00200.englishNames: pathogenic qi; Pathogenic Factors; Pathogenic qi
V012-N00200.definitions: Opposite to healthy qi, it is a collective term for all pathogenic factors.

V012-N00201.label: 阳邪
V012-N00201.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00201.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00201.englishNames: yang pathogen; Yang pathogens; pathogen attacking yang meridians/channels
V012-N00201.definitions: Opposite to yin pathogens, refers to pathogenic factors of yang nature, such as wind, summer-heat or fire-heat.

V012-N00202.label: 阴火
V012-N00202.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00202.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00202.definitions: The pathogenic fire produced from improper diet, fatigue, joy, anger, grief, overthinking, qi deficiency or yang deficiency.

V012-N00203.label: 阴邪
V012-N00203.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00203.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00203.englishNames: yin pathogen; pathogen attacking yin meridians/channels; Yin pathogens
V012-N00203.definitions: Opposite to yang pathogens, refers to pathogenic factors of yin nature, such as cold or dampness.

V012-N00204.label: 风为百病之长
V012-N00204.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00204.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00204.aliases: 风为百病之首； Wind is the spearhead of a hundred diseases
V012-N00204.englishNames: wind being primary pathogen; wind being leading cause of diseases; Wind is the leading cause of diseases
V012-N00204.pinyinNames: fēng wéi bǎi bìng zhī zhǎng
V012-N00204.definitions: Wind may cause a wide variety of conditions. It is the spearhead of exogenous pathogenic factors and liable to associate itself with cold, dampness, dryness and heat.

V012-N00205.label: 风善行数变
V012-N00205.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00205.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00205.englishNames: wind being mobile and changeable; Wind moves and changes swiftly
V012-N00205.definitions: Wind is characterised by swift changes. Diseases caused by wind are marked by a sudden onset of symptoms in moving locations.

V012-N00206.label: 风性主动
V012-N00206.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00206.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00206.englishNames: Wind causes movement; characteristic of wind being mobile; wind attributing to mobility
V012-N00206.definitions: Wind is characterized by constant moving. It causes the limbs to tremble.

V012-N00207.label: 风性开泄
V012-N00207.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00207.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00207.englishNames: wind pathogen being characterized by openingdispersing; wind attributing to opening and releasing; Wind opens and disperses
V012-N00207.definitions: Wind loosens the interspace between skin/muscle fibers and opens the skin pores, resulting in sweating and aversion to wind.

V012-N00208.label: 风性轻扬
V012-N00208.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00208.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00208.definitions: Wind is characterized by upward flowing. It easily affects the head, face, surface of the body and yang meridians.

V012-N00209.label: 风邪
V012-N00209.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00209.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00209.englishNames: wind pathogen; Pathogenic wind; Pathogenic Wind
V012-N00209.definitions: A pathogenic factor characterized by opening the skin pores, high mobility and upward/outward movement.

V012-N00210.label: 饮
V012-N00210.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00210.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00210.englishNames: Fluid retention; retained fluid; fluid retention; drink$decoction
V012-N00210.definitions: Fluid retention is relative to phlegm. It refers to the thin, clear pathological products that accumulate in the body due to disordered water metabolism. It can also be an etiological factor for a variety of diseases.

V012-N00211.label: 饮食不洁
V012-N00211.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00211.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00211.englishNames: contaminated food; Consumption of unclean food
V012-N00211.definitions: Intake of contaminated or poisonous food.

V012-N00212.label: 饮食不节
V012-N00212.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00212.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00212.englishNames: irregular eating; Improper Diet; Dietary irregularities; dietary irregularities
V012-N00212.pinyinNames: yin shi bu jie; yǐn shí bù jié
V012-N00212.definitions: Improper diet, including excessive hunger and binge eating.

V012-N00213.label: 饮食偏嗜
V012-N00213.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00213.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00213.definitions: Excessive indulgence in specific food, including cold or hot food, certain flavours or alcohol.

V012-N00214.label: 饮食失宜
V012-N00214.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病因类
V012-N00214.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00214.definitions: Diet harmful to health, including unhealthy dietary habits, ingestion of contaminated food, dietary preference, etc.

V012-N00215.label: 病机类
V012-N00215.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语
V012-N00215.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00216.label: 两感
V012-N00216.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00216.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00216.englishNames: dual contraction; Damage to paired meridians; double invasion; cold attack on paired channels
V012-N00216.definitions: A pathological state of pathogenic factors affecting two meridians that are interior–exteriorly connected.

V012-N00217.label: 亡阳
V012-N00217.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00217.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00217.aliases: Yang depletion； 阳脱； 脱阳
V012-N00217.englishNames: Yang collapse; yang depletion; yang exhaustion; yang collapse; yang prostration
V012-N00217.definitions: A critical state of massive loss and sudden failure of yang qi.

V012-N00218.label: 亡阴
V012-N00218.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00218.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00218.aliases: 阴脱； Yin depletion； 脱阴
V012-N00218.englishNames: yin prostration; Yin collapse; yin exhaustion; yin collapse; yin depletion
V012-N00218.definitions: A critical state of massive loss and sudden failure of yin fluid.

V012-N00219.label: 伏邪
V012-N00219.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00219.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00219.englishNames: latent pathogen; Latent pathogenic qi; hidden pathogen
V012-N00219.definitions: Opposite to exposure to acute pathogenic qi in the theory of febrile diseases, it causes the occurrence of diseases after a period of time.

V012-N00220.label: 传化
V012-N00220.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00220.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00220.englishNames: transmission and transformation; Transmission and transformation; (transmission and) transformation
V012-N00220.definitions: Transmission and transformation of diseases among five zang organs.

V012-N00221.label: 伤津
V012-N00221.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00221.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00221.englishNames: impairment of fluid; Damage to thin body fluid; damage to fluid
V012-N00221.definitions: A pathological state in which the thin, clear fluids are consumed.

V012-N00222.label: 关门不利
V012-N00222.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00222.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00222.englishNames: The water gate failing to operate
V012-N00222.definitions: A pathological state in which dysfunctions of the kidney in qi transformation causes inhibited urination (dysuria) and oedema.

V012-N00223.label: 劳复
V012-N00223.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00223.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00223.englishNames: Recurrence due to overwork; relapse due to overstrain; taxation relapse; recurrence caused by overexertion; overfatigue relapse
V012-N00223.definitions: Relapse of disease due to the damage to healthy qi by overwork

V012-N00224.label: 卒发
V012-N00224.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00224.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00224.definitions: A rapid appearance of symptoms after being in contact with a pathogen.

V012-N00225.label: 卫弱营强
V012-N00225.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00225.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00225.englishNames: weak defense qi and strong nutrient; weak defensive qi and strong nutrient qi; Weak Wei-defence and strong Ying-nutrients; weak defense with strong nutrient
V012-N00225.pinyinNames: wei ruo ying qiang; wèi ruò yíng qiáng
V012-N00225.definitions: A pathological state of spontaneous sweating without fever due to deficiency of the Wei-defensive qi (in an exterior pattern).

V012-N00226.label: 卫强营弱
V012-N00226.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00226.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00226.englishNames: strong defensive qi and weak nutrient qi; strong defense with weak nutrient; Strong Wei-defence and weak Ying-nutrients
V012-N00226.definitions: A pathological state of sweating that occurs only during fever in an exterior pattern.

V012-N00227.label: 合邪
V012-N00227.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00227.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00227.englishNames: combined pathogen; Combined pathogens; combined pathogens
V012-N00227.definitions: Two or more external pathogenic factors invade the body. Alternatively, it means exogenous pathogenic factors triggering previously existing diseases.

V012-N00228.label: 因虚致实
V012-N00228.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00228.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00228.englishNames: excess resulted from deficiency; Deficiency transforming into excess
V012-N00228.definitions: A transformation from a deficiency pattern (deficiency of healthy qi) to an excess pattern (hyperactivity of pathogenic factors).

V012-N00229.label: 壮火食气
V012-N00229.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00229.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00229.englishNames: hyperactive fire consuming qi; Hyperactive fire consuming qi
V012-N00229.definitions: A pathological state of yin–yang disharmony and hyperactive yang transforming into fire. Alternatively, it occurs when prolonged intake of pungent, warm-property aphrodisiacs generates internal fire toxins and consumes essence qi.

V012-N00230.label: 复发
V012-N00230.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00230.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00230.definitions: Relapse of diseases due to etiological or inducing factors.

V012-N00231.label: 实
V012-N00231.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00231.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00231.definitions: Opposite to deficiency, it is a hyperactive pathological state due to a fierce struggle between excessive pathogenic factors and normal healthy qi.

V012-N00232.label: 实中夹虚
V012-N00232.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00232.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00232.englishNames: excess complicated with deficiency; excess with deficiency complication; Excess complicated with deficiency; excess complicated by deficiency
V012-N00232.pinyinNames: shí zhōng jiā xū; shi zhong jia xu
V012-N00232.definitions: A pathological state that mainly manifests as excess of pathogenic factors, accompanied by a deficiency of healthy qi.

V012-N00233.label: 徐发
V012-N00233.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00233.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00233.englishNames: gradual onset; gradual occurrence; insidious onset; Gradual onset
V012-N00233.definitions: A gradual appearance of symptoms after being in contact with a pathogen.

V012-N00234.label: 新感
V012-N00234.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00234.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00234.englishNames: latest contraction; new affection; New contraction
V012-N00234.definitions: Opposite to the latent pathogenic qi in the theory of warm disease, it often causes a sudden onset of disease.

V012-N00235.label: 正气
V012-N00235.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00235.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00235.englishNames: healthy qi; vital qi; Healthy qi
V012-N00235.definitions: Opposite to pathogenic factors, it is a collective term for physiological functions of the body, including the body’s ability to adapt to the external environment, defend against pathogens and recover from diseases.

V012-N00236.label: 正邪相争
V012-N00236.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00236.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00236.englishNames: struggle between vital qi and pathogen; Struggle between anti-pathogenic qi and pathogenic factors; struggle between the healthy qi and pathogenic qi; struggle between healthy qi and pathogenic qi; Struggle between Vital Qi and Pathogen
V012-N00236.pinyinNames: zhèng xié xiāng zhēng; zheng xie xiang zheng
V012-N00236.definitions: The struggle between healthy qi and pathogenic factors determines the pathogenic nature of diseases. Alternatively, it is specifically used to explain the pathogenesis of alternating fever and chills in Shaoyang disease due to cold damage.

V012-N00237.label: 气不化津
V012-N00237.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00237.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00237.englishNames: Qi failing to transform thin fluids; qi failing to form fluid
V012-N00237.definitions: A pathological state in which yang qi fails to vaporize water and perform qi transformation.

V012-N00238.label: 气不摄血
V012-N00238.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00238.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00238.englishNames: Qi failing to contain blood; failure of qi to control blood; qi failing to control blood
V012-N00238.pinyinNames: qì bú shè xuè; qi bu she xue
V012-N00238.definitions: A pathological state in which qi fails to contain blood and allows the blood to flow out of the vessels.

V012-N00239.label: 气机不利
V012-N00239.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00239.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00239.englishNames: disturbance of qi movement; Impaired qi activity; disorder of qi movement; inhibited qi movement
V012-N00239.pinyinNames: qi ji bu li; qì jī bú lì
V012-N00239.definitions: A general term for dysfunctions of the zang–fu organs. It is often used to explain dysfunctions of qi of the zang–fu organs in ascending the clear and descending the turbid. It may cause hiccups, chest stuffiness, abdominal distension/pain and irregular bowel/bladder movements.

V012-N00240.label: 气机失调
V012-N00240.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00240.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00240.englishNames: Disordered qi activity; qi movement disorder
V012-N00240.definitions: Disordered activity of qi in ascending, descending, entering and exiting. It may cause qi stagnation, adverse qi flow, qi sinking, qi blockage and qi exhaustion.

V012-N00241.label: 气滞
V012-N00241.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00241.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00241.englishNames: qi stagnation; Qi impediment; qi stegnation
V012-N00241.definitions: A pathological state in which the movement of qi is inhibited.

V012-N00242.label: 气滞血瘀
V012-N00242.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00242.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00242.englishNames: qi stagntion and blood stasis; qi stagnation and blood stasis; blood stasis due to qi stagnation; Blood stasis due to qi stagnation; stagnation of qi and blood stasis
V012-N00242.pinyinNames: qi zhi xue yu; qì zhì xuè yū
V012-N00242.definitions: A pathological state in which stagnant qi affects the circulation of blood and causes simultaneous presence of qi stagnation and blood stasis.

V012-N00243.label: 气病及血
V012-N00243.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00243.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00244.label: 气脱
V012-N00244.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00244.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00244.englishNames: qi desertion; qi collapse; qi prostration; Qi collapse
V012-N00244.definitions: A pathological state in which extreme deficiency of qi causes qi to float on the exterior of the body.

V012-N00245.label: 气虚
V012-N00245.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00245.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00245.definitions: A pathological state of decreased zang–fu functions or body weakness due to deficiency of qi.

V012-N00246.label: 气虚血瘀
V012-N00246.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00246.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00246.englishNames: Blood stasis due to qi deficiency; qi deficiency with blood stasis; qi deficiency and blood stasis; blood stasis due to qi deficiency
V012-N00246.pinyinNames: qì xū xuè yū; qi xu xue yu
V012-N00246.definitions: A pathological state in which qi fails to promote the circulation of blood and causes blood stasis, resulting in simultaneous presence of qi deficiency and blood stasis.

V012-N00247.label: 气血两虚
V012-N00247.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00247.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00247.englishNames: Dual deficiency of qi and blood; deficiency of both qi and blood; dual deficiency of qi and blood
V012-N00247.definitions: A pathological state of malnourishment of the body or weakened bodily function due to simultaneous presence of qi deficiency and blood deficiency.

V012-N00248.label: 气血失调
V012-N00248.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00248.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00248.englishNames: disorder of qi and blood; disharmony of qi and blood; Qi-blood disharmony
V012-N00248.pinyinNames: qì xuè shī tiáo; qi xue shi tiao
V012-N00248.definitions: Any failure in the mutually coordinating relationship between qi and blood.

V012-N00249.label: 气逆
V012-N00249.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00249.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00249.englishNames: qi counterflow; reversed flow of qi; Qi counterflow
V012-N00249.definitions: A pathological state of reversed upflow of qi. Either excessive upward flow of qi or inhibited downward flow of qi.

V012-N00250.label: 气郁
V012-N00250.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00250.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00250.definitions: Stagnant movement of qi. It is often associated with emotions and disorder of qi and blood. Often refers to liver qi stagnation.

V012-N00251.label: 气郁化火
V012-N00251.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00251.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00251.englishNames: transformation of qi depression into fire; stagnant qi transforming into fire; Qi stagnation transforming into fire; qi depression transforming into fire
V012-N00251.pinyinNames: qì yù huà huǒ; qi yu hua huo
V012-N00251.definitions: A pathological state of prolonged qi stagnation transforming into fire heat.

V012-N00252.label: 气闭
V012-N00252.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00252.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00252.englishNames: qi blockage; Qi blockage; qi block
V012-N00252.definitions: A pathological state in which qi is obstructed inside.

V012-N00253.label: 气阴两虚
V012-N00253.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00253.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00253.englishNames: deficiency of both qi and yin; dual deficiency of qi and yin; Dual deficiency of qi and yin
V012-N00253.pinyinNames: qi yin liang xu; qì yīn liǎng xū
V012-N00253.definitions: A pathological state of simultaneous presence of qi deficiency and yin deficiency.

V012-N00254.label: 气陷
V012-N00254.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00254.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00254.englishNames: Qi sinking; qi sinking; qi fall
V012-N00254.definitions: A pathological state in which qi fails to lift or hold but flows downward.

V012-N00255.label: 气随液脱
V012-N00255.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00255.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00255.englishNames: depletion of fluid involving qi desertion; Qi collapse following loss of thick fluids; qi prostration following liquid depletion; qi collapse due to humor depletion
V012-N00255.definitions: A critical state in which a massive loss of bodily fluids causes sudden collapse/loss of yang qi.

V012-N00256.label: 气随血脱
V012-N00256.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00256.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00256.englishNames: qi prostration following blood loss; Qi collapse following heavy blood loss; qi collapse following blood loss; qi desertion due to blood depletion; qi collapse following bleeding
V012-N00256.pinyinNames: qì suí xuè tuō; qi sui xue tuo
V012-N00256.definitions: A critical state in which massive blood loss causes sudden collapse of qi.

V012-N00257.label: 水停气阻
V012-N00257.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00257.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00257.englishNames: water retention with qi obstruction; water retention due to obstruction of qi; Water retention obstructing the flow of qi
V012-N00257.pinyinNames: shui ting qi zu; shuǐ tíng qì zǔ
V012-N00257.definitions: A pathological state in which retained water within the body impedes the qi ascending, descending and transformation of the zang–fu organs.

V012-N00258.label: 津枯血燥
V012-N00258.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00258.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00258.englishNames: fluid consumption and blood dryness; fluid exhaustion with blood dryness; fluid exhaustion and blood dryness; depletion of fluid causing blood dryness; Fluid exhaustion leading to blood dryness
V012-N00258.pinyinNames: jin ku xue zao; jīn kū xuè zào
V012-N00258.definitions: A pathological state in which fluids fail to supplement blood and causes blood dryness and internal dry heat.

V012-N00259.label: 由实转虚
V012-N00259.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00259.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00259.englishNames: deficiency transformed from excess; conversion of excess into deficiency; Excess transforming into deficiency; transformation from excess into deficiency
V012-N00259.pinyinNames: you shi zhuan xu; yóu shí zhuǎn xū
V012-N00259.definitions: A transformation from an excess pattern (hyperactivity of pathogenic factors) to a deficiency pattern (deficiency of healthy qi).

V012-N00260.label: 病机
V012-N00260.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00260.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00260.definitions: The mechanism of the occurrence, progress, and change of the disease.

V012-N00261.label: 相火妄动
V012-N00261.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00261.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00261.aliases: Frenetic stirring of the ministerial fire
V012-N00261.englishNames: hyperactivity of ministerial fire; frenetic stirring of the ministerial fire; frenetic stirring of ministerial fire; Reckless stirring of ministerial fire
V012-N00261.pinyinNames: xiàng huǒ wàng dòng; xiang huo wang dong
V012-N00261.definitions: A pathological state of upward flame of fire due to yin deficiency of the liver and kidney. Common symptoms may include dizziness, headache, blurred vision, tinnitus, deafness, irritability, dream-disturbed sleep, feverish sensations in the palms, soles and chest, hypersexuality, nocturnal emissions, premature ejaculations, etc.

V012-N00262.label: 真实假虚
V012-N00262.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00262.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00262.englishNames: true excess disease with false deficient manifestation; true excess with false deficiency; True excess with false deficiency
V012-N00262.pinyinNames: zhen shi jia xu; zhēn shí jiǎ xū
V012-N00262.definitions: The false deficiency symptoms due to extreme excess of pathogenic factors.

V012-N00263.label: 真虚假实
V012-N00263.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00263.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00263.englishNames: true deficiency disease with false excessive manifestation; true deficiency with false excess; True deficiency with false excess
V012-N00263.pinyinNames: zhēn xū jiǎ shí; zhen xu jia shi
V012-N00263.definitions: The false excess symptoms due to extreme deficiency of healthy qi.

V012-N00264.label: 脱液
V012-N00264.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00264.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00264.englishNames: Exhaustion of thick body fluid; collapse of liquid
V012-N00264.definitions: A pathological state of emaciation and functional failures of the zang–fu organs. Alternatively, it can be a life-threatening sign due to major consumption of heavy, thick fluids.

V012-N00265.label: 营卫不和
V012-N00265.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00265.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00265.englishNames: nutrient-defense disharmony; Disharmony between the Ying-nutrients and Wei-defence; disharmony between nutrient qi and defensive qi; disharmony of nutrient-defensive qi; disharmony between nutrient and defensive qi
V012-N00265.pinyinNames: yíng wèi bù hé; ying wei bu he
V012-N00265.definitions: A pathological state of lack of coordination between the Ying-nutritive qi and Wei-defensive qi.

V012-N00266.label: 虚
V012-N00266.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00266.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00266.definitions: Opposite to excess, it is a hypoactive pathological state due to the struggle between weak healthy qi and mild/moderate pathogenic factors.

V012-N00267.label: 虚中夹实
V012-N00267.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00267.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00267.englishNames: deficiency complicated with excess; deficiency complicated by excess; deficiency with excess complication; Deficiency complicated with excess
V012-N00267.definitions: A pathological state that mainly manifests as a deficiency of healthy qi, accompanied by excessive pathogenic factors.

V012-N00268.label: 虚实真假
V012-N00268.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00268.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00268.englishNames: true and false manifestation of deficiency and excess; True or false deficiency and excess; true or false deficiency-excess; true-false of excess-deficiency; true or false excess and deficiency
V012-N00268.pinyinNames: xu shi zhen jia; xū shí zhēn jiǎ
V012-N00268.definitions: The false symptoms contrary to the true nature of the disease. This includes true deficiency with false excess and true excess with false deficiency.

V012-N00269.label: 虚实转化
V012-N00269.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00269.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00269.englishNames: Deficiency–excess transformation; transformation of deficiency and excess
V012-N00269.definitions: The mutual transformation between deficiency of healthy qi and excess of pathogenic factors. This includes excess transforming into deficiency and deficiency into excess.

V012-N00270.label: 虚实错杂
V012-N00270.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00270.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00270.aliases: 虚实夹杂； Mixed deficiency and excess
V012-N00270.englishNames: Combined deficiency and excess; deficiency and excess in complexity
V012-N00270.definitions: A pathological state due to deficiency of healthy qi and excess of pathogenic factors. This includes deficiency complicated with excess and excess complicated with deficiency.

V012-N00271.label: 虚阳上浮
V012-N00271.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00271.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00271.englishNames: deficiency yang floating upward; Floating upward of deficiency yang; deficient yang with upper manifestation; rising of deficiency-yang
V012-N00271.definitions: A pathological state of upward floating of yang qi due to excessive yin rejecting yang or loss/consumption of essence blood.

V012-N00272.label: 血不养筋
V012-N00272.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00272.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00272.englishNames: Blood failing to nourish the sinews; failure of blood to nourish tendons
V012-N00272.definitions: A pathological state in which liver blood fails to nourish the sinews and causes muscle/tendon spasm.

V012-N00273.label: 血不归经
V012-N00273.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00273.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00273.englishNames: Blood failing to flow within the vessels; blood flow outside channels; blood failing to stay in the meridians
V012-N00273.definitions: A pathological state in which blood moves recklessly out of the vessels.

V012-N00274.label: 血寒
V012-N00274.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00274.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00274.englishNames: Blood cold; blood cold; cold in blood
V012-N00274.definitions: A pathological state in which cold enters the blood phase and causes the blood to stagnate or coagulate.

V012-N00275.label: 血热
V012-N00275.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00275.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00275.englishNames: blood heat; heat in blood; Blood heat
V012-N00275.definitions: A pathological state in which heat enters the blood phase and causes the blood to move recklessly.

V012-N00276.label: 血热妄行
V012-N00276.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00276.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00276.englishNames: frenetic movement of blood due to heat; Reckless flow of blood due to heat
V012-N00276.definitions: A pathological state of bleeding due to heat in the blood stirring or consuming blood and forcing it out of the vessels.

V012-N00277.label: 血瘀
V012-N00277.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00277.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00277.definitions: A pathological state of slow, coagulated or stagnant circulation of blood.

V012-N00278.label: 血虚
V012-N00278.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00278.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00278.definitions: A pathological state in which deficient blood fails to nourish the zang–fu organs and meridians/channels.

V012-N00279.label: 血随气逆
V012-N00279.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00279.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00279.englishNames: bleeding due to qi reversed flow; blood flowing counterflow with qi; bleeding following qi counterflow; Blood counterflow with qi
V012-N00279.pinyinNames: xuè suí qì nì; xue sui qi ni
V012-N00279.definitions: A pathological state in which upward adverse flow of qi leads to blood rushing upward and results in haematemesis, haemoptysis or stroke.

V012-N00280.label: 邪害空窍
V012-N00280.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00280.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00280.englishNames: Pathogenic factors attacking orifices; pathogens affecting facial orifices
V012-N00280.pinyinNames: xié hài kōng qiào; xie hai kong qiao
V012-N00280.definitions: Pathological states caused by pathogenic factors invading the mouth, nose, ears and eyes.

V012-N00281.label: 邪正盛衰
V012-N00281.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00281.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00281.englishNames: exuberance and debilitation of the healthy qi or pathogenic qi; rising and falling of vital qi and pathogen; exuberance or decline of healthy and pathogenic qi; Waxing and waning of anti-pathogenic qi and pathogenic factors
V012-N00281.definitions: The pathological states due to the struggle between healthy qi and pathogenic factors.

V012-N00282.label: 阳损及阴
V012-N00282.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00282.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00282.englishNames: yang deficiency involving yin; yang impairment affecting yin; Deficiency of yang affecting yin; detriment to yang affects yin; yang injury with yin involved
V012-N00282.pinyinNames: yang sun ji yin; yáng sǔn jí yīn
V012-N00282.definitions: A pathological state in which yang qi deficiency impedes the generation of yin, resulting in deficiency of both yin and yang with a preponderance of yang deficiency.

V012-N00283.label: 阳盛格阴
V012-N00283.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00283.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00283.aliases: Excessive yang repelling yin； 阳极似阴
V012-N00283.englishNames: Excessive yang rejecting yin; exberant yang repelling yin; exuberant yang repelling yin
V012-N00283.pinyinNames: yáng shèng gé yīn; yang sheng ge yin
V012-N00283.definitions: A pathological state of true heat with false cold when interior yang heat blocks the movement of qi and forces the yin to float on the exterior of the body.

V012-N00284.label: 阳盛阴衰
V012-N00284.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00284.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00284.englishNames: Yang waxing with yin waning; exuberance of yang with decline of yin; yang exuberance with yin debilitation
V012-N00284.pinyinNames: yáng shèng yīn shuāi; yang sheng yin shuai
V012-N00284.definitions: Coexisting yang waxing and yin waning. Often develops when waxing yang severely damages yin; however, yin damage is more severe.

V012-N00285.label: 阳胜则热
V012-N00285.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00285.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00285.englishNames: predominance of yang leading to heat; Yang excess leading to heat
V012-N00285.definitions: A pathological state of overwhelming yang leading to excess heat.

V012-N00286.label: 阳胜则阴病
V012-N00286.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00286.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00286.englishNames: predominance of yang making yin suffer; When yang prevails, yin fluids will be consumed.; predominance of yang leading to disorder of yin
V012-N00286.pinyinNames: yáng shèng zé yīn bìng; yang sheng ze yin bing
V012-N00286.definitions: A pathological state of overwhelming yang heat consuming yin fluids.

V012-N00287.label: 阳虚则寒
V012-N00287.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00287.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00287.englishNames: yang deficiency leading to cold; Yang deficiency leading to cold
V012-N00287.pinyinNames: yáng xū zé hán; yang xu ze han
V012-N00287.definitions: A pathological state of yang qi deficiency leading to deficiency cold.

V012-N00288.label: 阳虚阴盛
V012-N00288.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00288.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00288.englishNames: Yang deficiency with yin excess; yang deficiency with yin exuberance; deficiency of yang with decline of yin
V012-N00288.pinyinNames: yáng xū yīn shèng; yang xu yin sheng
V012-N00288.definitions: Mutual restraint between yin and yang maintains relative yin–yang equilibrium. The failure of yang to restrain yin may cause relative yin excess.

V012-N00289.label: 阴损及阳
V012-N00289.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00289.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00289.englishNames: yin impairment affecting yang; Impairment of yin affecting yang; yin deficiency involving yang; yin injury with yang involved; detriment to yin affects yang
V012-N00289.pinyinNames: yin sun ji yang; yīn sǔn jí yáng
V012-N00289.definitions: A pathological state in which consumption of yin affects yang qi, resulting in deficiency of both yin and yang with a preponderance of yin deficiency.

V012-N00290.label: 阴盛格阳
V012-N00290.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00290.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00290.aliases: Excessive yin repelling yang； 格阳； 阴极似阳
V012-N00290.englishNames: Excessive yin rejecting yang; exuberant yin repalling yang; exuberant yin repelling yang
V012-N00290.pinyinNames: yīn shèng gé yáng; yin sheng ge yang
V012-N00290.definitions: A pathological state of true cold with false heat when interior yin cold due to extreme yang deficiency forces the deficient yang to float on the exterior of the body.

V012-N00291.label: 阴盛阳衰
V012-N00291.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00291.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00291.englishNames: yin exuberance with yang debilitation; Yin waxing with yang waning; exuberant yin and declined yang
V012-N00291.pinyinNames: yīn shèng yáng shuāi; yin sheng yang shuai
V012-N00291.definitions: Coexisting yin waxing and yang waning. Over time, excess yin cold may damage yang qi and result in yang depletion.

V012-N00292.label: 阴竭阳脱
V012-N00292.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00292.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00292.englishNames: yin exhaustion and yang collapse; Yin exhaustion and yang collapse; exhaustion of yin and collapse of yang; depletion of yin causing yang collapse
V012-N00292.pinyinNames: yin jie yang tuo; yīn jié yáng tuō
V012-N00292.definitions: A pathological state of exhaustion of yin fluid and loss of yang qi.

V012-N00293.label: 阴胜则寒
V012-N00293.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00293.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00293.englishNames: predominance of yin leading to cold; Yin excess leading to cold
V012-N00293.definitions: A pathological state of overwhelming yin leading to excess cold.

V012-N00294.label: 阴胜则阳病
V012-N00294.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00294.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00294.englishNames: predominance of yin leading to disorder of yang; When yin prevails, yang qi will be impaired.; predominance of yin making yang suffer
V012-N00294.pinyinNames: yin sheng ze yang bing; yīn shèng zé yáng bìng
V012-N00294.definitions: A pathological state of excess yin cold consuming yang qi.

V012-N00295.label: 阴虚则热
V012-N00295.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00295.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00295.englishNames: yin deficiency leading to heat; Yin deficiency leading to heat
V012-N00295.pinyinNames: yin xu ze re; yīn xū zé rè
V012-N00295.definitions: A pathological state in which yin fluid fails to control yang and causes a relative hyperactivity of yang and deficiency heat.

V012-N00296.label: 阴虚火旺
V012-N00296.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00296.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00296.englishNames: yin deficiency resulting in rampant fire; yin deficiency with effulgent fire; hyperactivity of fire due to yin deficiency; Yin deficiency leading to fire hyperactivity
V012-N00296.pinyinNames: yin xu huo wang; yīn xū huǒ wàng
V012-N00296.definitions: A pathological state in which yin fluid fails to control yang and causes a relative hyperactivity of yang and deficiency fire.

V012-N00297.label: 阴虚阳亢
V012-N00297.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00297.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00297.englishNames: yin deficiency with yang hyperactivity; Yin deficiency leading to yang hyperactivity
V012-N00297.pinyinNames: yin xu yang kang; yīn xū yáng kàng
V012-N00297.definitions: A pathological state in which yin fluid fails to control yang and causes a relative hyperactivity of yang.

V012-N00298.label: 阴阳两虚
V012-N00298.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00298.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00298.englishNames: deficiency of both yin and yang; deficiency of both yin sand yang; dual deficiency of yin and yang; Dual deficiency of yin and yang
V012-N00298.pinyinNames: yin yang liang xu; yīn yáng liǎng xū
V012-N00298.definitions: A pathological state of coexisting yin deficiency and yang deficiency.

V012-N00299.label: 阴阳互损
V012-N00299.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00299.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00299.englishNames: yin or yang injury with yang or yin involved; Mutual impairment between yin and yang
V012-N00299.definitions: A pathological state of deficiency of both yin and yang. Often results from severe deficiency of yin affecting yang or vice versa. This includes yin deficiency affecting yang and yang deficiency affecting yin.

V012-N00300.label: 阴阳亡失
V012-N00300.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00300.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00300.englishNames: Collapse of yin or yang; prostration of yin and yang
V012-N00300.definitions: A critical state of sudden, heavy loss of yin fluid or yang qi. This includes yin collapse and yang collapse.

V012-N00301.label: 阴阳偏盛
V012-N00301.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00301.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00301.englishNames: abnormal exuberance of yin or yang; excess of either yin or yang; Excess yin or yang; preponderance of yin or yang
V012-N00301.pinyinNames: yin yang pian sheng; yīn yáng piān shèng
V012-N00301.definitions: Pathological states due to excess of yin or yang.

V012-N00302.label: 阴阳偏衰
V012-N00302.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00302.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00302.englishNames: deficiency of either yin or yang; abnormal debilitation of yin or yang; Waning of yin or yang; decline of yin or yang
V012-N00302.pinyinNames: yin yang pian shuai; yīn yáng piān shuāi
V012-N00302.definitions: Pathological states due to deficiency of yin or yang.

V012-N00303.label: 阴阳失调
V012-N00303.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00303.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00303.englishNames: Disharmony between yin and yang; yinyang disharmony; imbalance between yin and yang; yin-yang disharmony
V012-N00303.pinyinNames: yīn yáng shī tiáo; yin yang shi tiao
V012-N00303.definitions: A general term for pathological states due to waxing and waning of yin and yang. This includes excess of yin or yang, deficiency of yin or yang, mutual impairment between yin and yang, rejection between yin and yang, transformation between yin and yang, and depletion/loss of yin or yang.

V012-N00304.label: 阴阳格拒
V012-N00304.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00304.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00304.englishNames: Yin–yang rejection; yin-yang repulsion; repelling of yin-yang; expulsion of yinyang
V012-N00304.definitions: A serious pathological state in which extremely excessive yin in the interior forces the deficient yang to spread outward or extremely excessive yang in the interior keeps deficient yin on the outside, forming pseudo-heat or pseudo-cold. This includes excessive yin rejecting yang and excessive yang rejecting yin.

V012-N00305.label: 阴阳离决
V012-N00305.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00305.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00305.englishNames: Yin–yang separation; dissociation of yin and yang; divorce of yin-yang; separation between yin and yang
V012-N00305.definitions: A critical state of separation between yin and yang due to extreme deficiency of yin and yang.

V012-N00306.label: 食复
V012-N00306.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜病机类
V012-N00306.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00306.englishNames: relapse due to dietary irregularity; Recurrence due to improper diet; relapse due to improper diet
V012-N00306.definitions: Relapse of diseases due to intake of improper diet or intake of contaminated food.

V012-N00307.label: 精气血津液类
V012-N00307.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语
V012-N00307.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00307.englishNames: Essence, qi, blood and body fluids

V012-N00308.label: 中气
V012-N00308.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00308.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00308.definitions: It is also known as qi of the spleen and stomach.

V012-N00309.label: 元气
V012-N00309.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00309.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00309.definitions: Rooted in the kidney, Yuan-primordial qi consists of innate essence qi and Ying-nutrients qi transformed from water and food. It includes primordial yin qi and primordial yang qi.

V012-N00310.label: 先天之气
V012-N00310.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00310.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00310.aliases: Pre-heaven qi； prenatal qi； congenital qi
V012-N00310.definitions: The qi inherited from one’s parents and stored in the kidney.

V012-N00311.label: 先天之精
V012-N00311.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00311.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00311.aliases: Pre-heaven essence； prenatal essence； congenital essence
V012-N00311.definitions: The original substance of life inherited from one’s parents and contributed to the generation of offspring.

V012-N00312.label: 升降出入
V012-N00312.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00312.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00312.definitions: The basic forms of qi movement.

V012-N00313.label: 卫气
V012-N00313.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00313.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00313.definitions: Opposite to the Ying-nutrient qi. It travels outside the vessels and is transformed from the nutrients of water and food.

V012-N00314.label: 卫行脉外
V012-N00314.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00314.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00314.definitions: Wei-defence is yang and resolute in nature. It travels outside the vessels.

V012-N00315.label: 后天之气
V012-N00315.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00315.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00315.definitions: The qi transformed from the essential qi of water and food as well as the clean qi (fresh air inhaled in the lung) of the nature.

V012-N00316.label: 后天之精
V012-N00316.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00316.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00316.definitions: The nutritive substances obtained through qi transformation of the zang–fu organs after birth.

V012-N00317.label: 宗气
V012-N00317.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00317.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00317.definitions: The qi that accumulates in the chest. It consists of clean qi (fresh air inhaled in the lung) in the nature and qi transformed from water and food.

V012-N00318.label: 少火
V012-N00318.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00318.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00318.definitions: The normal fire that benefits growth.

V012-N00319.label: 少火生气
V012-N00319.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00319.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00319.definitions: The normal fire generates the Yuan-primordial qi and maintains vital activities.

V012-N00320.label: 气
V012-N00320.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00320.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00320.definitions: The intangible, high-mobility nutritive substance that maintains vital activities.

V012-N00321.label: 气为血之帅
V012-N00321.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00321.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00321.englishNames: Qi is the commander of the blood
V012-N00321.definitions: This refers to the functions of qi in transforming, circulating and governing blood.

V012-N00322.label: 气主煦之
V012-N00322.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00322.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00322.definitions: Qi has the function of warming the body.

V012-N00323.label: 气化
V012-N00323.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00323.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00323.definitions: A general term referring to various changes generated by qi movement. All things in the universe are transformed by qi.

V012-N00324.label: 气机
V012-N00324.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00324.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00324.definitions: Qi movement can be classified into four basic forms, namely ascending, descending, exiting and entering.

V012-N00325.label: 气能摄津
V012-N00325.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00325.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00325.aliases: Qi holds body fluids； qi contains body fluids
V012-N00325.definitions: Qi keeps the body fluid within the waterways and maintains its normal circulation.

V012-N00326.label: 气能摄血
V012-N00326.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00326.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00326.aliases: Qi holds blood； Qi contains blood
V012-N00326.definitions: This refers to the normal functioning of spleen qi and liver qi in keeping the blood circulating within the blood vessels.

V012-N00327.label: 气能生津
V012-N00327.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00327.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00327.definitions: Qi of the zang–fu organs transforms into and engenders body fluids.

V012-N00328.label: 气能生血
V012-N00328.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00328.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00328.definitions: Qi can transform into blood. Blood is transformed from nutrients from water and food and Ying-nutrient qi within the blood vessels.

V012-N00329.label: 气能行津
V012-N00329.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00329.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00329.definitions: Qi of the zang–fu organs promotes the normal distribution of body fluids.

V012-N00330.label: 气能行血
V012-N00330.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00330.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00330.definitions: The heart qi, lung qi and liver qi maintain the normal circulation of blood.

V012-N00331.label: 津
V012-N00331.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00331.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00331.definitions: The thin, clear and high-mobility body fluids that distribute over the skin, muscles or orifices and permeates into blood vessels. This part of fluids moistens the body.

V012-N00332.label: 津液
V012-N00332.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00332.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00332.definitions: A collective term for thinner part of fluids (jin) and thicker part of liquid (ye). Body fluids are essential to maintain life activities.

V012-N00333.label: 津能载气
V012-N00333.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00333.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00333.definitions: The function of tangible body fluids in carrying intangible qi.

V012-N00334.label: 津血同源
V012-N00334.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00334.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00334.englishNames: Body fluids and blood share the same source
V012-N00334.definitions: Body fluids and blood are both transformed from nutrients found in water and food; the two mutually generate each other.

V012-N00335.label: 液
V012-N00335.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00335.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00335.definitions: The thick, low-mobility body fluids that perfuse into the bones, joints, zang–fu organs, brain and marrows. This part of fluids nourishes the body and lubricates the bones/joints.

V012-N00336.label: 真气
V012-N00336.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00336.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00336.definitions: A collective term for qi of the entire body, including innate qi and acquired qi.

V012-N00337.label: 精
V012-N00337.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00337.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00337.definitions: All tangible nutrients of the human body. It can also specifically refer to the kidney essence.

V012-N00338.label: 精气
V012-N00338.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00338.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00338.definitions: A collective term for essence and qi. It serves as the fundamental substance of the human body and maintains vital activities.

V012-N00339.label: 精血同源
V012-N00339.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00339.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00339.englishNames: Essence and blood share the same source
V012-N00339.definitions: Essence qi and blood are both transformed from nutrients of water and food; the two mutually generate each other.

V012-N00340.label: 经气
V012-N00340.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00340.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00340.definitions: The qi circulates within the meridians and falls under the category of healthy qi.

V012-N00341.label: 脏腑之气
V012-N00341.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00341.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00341.definitions: The qi stored in the zang–fu organs and maintains normal physiological functions of the zang–fu organs.

V012-N00342.label: 营气
V012-N00342.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00342.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00342.definitions: Opposite to Wei-defensive qi. Ying-nutrients qi is transformed from water and food and travels within the blood vessels.

V012-N00343.label: 营血
V012-N00343.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00343.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00343.definitions: The qi within blood and yin within qi. When used in combination with blood, it often represents yin blood. Alternatively, it generally refers to blood.

V012-N00344.label: 营行脉中
V012-N00344.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00344.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00344.definitions: Ying-nutrient is yin and flexible in nature and travels within the vessels.

V012-N00345.label: 血
V012-N00345.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00345.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00345.definitions: The red liquid that circulates within the blood vessels to moisten and nourish the body. It is an essential substance to maintain life activities.

V012-N00346.label: 血为气之母
V012-N00346.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00346.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00346.englishNames: Blood is the mother of qi
V012-N00346.definitions: Blood transforms into and carries qi.

V012-N00347.label: 血主濡之
V012-N00347.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00347.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00347.definitions: Blood has the function of nourishing and moistening the body.

V012-N00348.label: 血汗同源
V012-N00348.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00348.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00348.englishNames: Blood and sweat share the same source
V012-N00348.definitions: Since blood is composed of body fluids and Ying-nutrient qi and sweat is transformed from body fluids, the blood and sweat share the same source.

V012-N00349.label: 血能载气
V012-N00349.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜精气血津液类
V012-N00349.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00349.definitions: The function of tangible blood in carrying intangible qi.

V012-N00350.label: 藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00350.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语
V012-N00350.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00351.label: 三焦
V012-N00351.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00351.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00351.definitions: A fu organ. A collective term for upper jiao, middle jiao and lower jiao. It serves as a pathway for qi and water circulation and dominates qi transformation.

V012-N00352.label: 上焦
V012-N00352.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00352.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00352.definitions: The upper jiao is located above the diaphragm and includes the heart and lung.

V012-N00353.label: 上焦如雾
V012-N00353.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00353.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00353.definitions: The heart and lung distribute qi and blood. Their function is compared with the permeation of mist and dew.

V012-N00354.label: 下焦
V012-N00354.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00354.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00354.definitions: The lower jiao is located below the navel and includes the kidney, urinary bladder, large intestine, and small intestine.

V012-N00355.label: 下焦如渎
V012-N00355.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00355.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00355.definitions: The kidney and urinary bladder regulate water discharge. Their function is compared to an unobstructed ditch.

V012-N00356.label: 中正之官
V012-N00356.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00356.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00356.aliases: The office of impartiality and rectitude
V012-N00356.definitions: Refers to the gallbladder. The gallbladder is involved with judgment and decision-making. It is therefore compared to the role of justice.

V012-N00357.label: 中焦
V012-N00357.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00357.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00357.definitions: The middle jiao is located between the diaphragm and navel and includes the spleen, stomach, liver and gallbladder.

V012-N00358.label: 中焦如沤
V012-N00358.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00358.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00358.definitions: The spleen and stomach decompose water and food. Their function is compared to fermentation.

V012-N00359.label: 中精之府
V012-N00359.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00359.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00359.definitions: Refers to the gallbladder. The gallbladder stores bile.

V012-N00360.label: 五脏
V012-N00360.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00360.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00360.definitions: A collective term for the five internal organs—the heart, liver, spleen/pancreas, lung and kidney.

V012-N00361.label: 五脏化液
V012-N00361.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00361.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00361.englishNames: Fluids of the five zang organs
V012-N00361.definitions: The fluid of the heart is sweat; the fluid of the lung is nasal mucus; the fluid of the spleen is thin saliva; the fluid of the liver is tears; and the fluid of the kidney is the thick saliva.

V012-N00362.label: 五脏在志
V012-N00362.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00362.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00362.englishNames: Emotions of the five zang organs
V012-N00362.definitions: The emotion of the heart is joy; the emotion of the lung is grief; the emotion of the spleen is overthinking; the emotion of the liver is anger; and the emotion of the kidney is fear.

V012-N00363.label: 五脏外华
V012-N00363.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00363.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00363.englishNames: Lustres of the five zang organs
V012-N00363.definitions: The lustre of the heart shows in the face; the lustre of the lung shows in the skin hair; the lustre of the spleen shows in the lips; the lustre of the liver shows in the nails; and the lustre of the kidney shows in the hair.

V012-N00364.label: 五脏开窍
V012-N00364.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00364.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00364.englishNames: Opening of the five zang organs
V012-N00364.definitions: The heart opens into the tongue; the lung opens into the nose; the spleen opens into the mouth; the liver opens into the eyes; and the kidney opens into the ears.

V012-N00365.label: 五脏所主
V012-N00365.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00365.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00365.englishNames: Governance of the five zang organs
V012-N00365.definitions: The heart governs blood vessels; the lung governs the skin; the spleen governs the flesh; the liver governs the sinew/tendon; and the kidney governs the bones.

V012-N00366.label: 五脏所恶
V012-N00366.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00366.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00366.englishNames: Dislikes of the five zang organs
V012-N00366.definitions: The heart dislikes heat; the lung dislikes cold; the spleen dislikes dampness; the liver dislikes wind; and the kidney dislikes dryness.

V012-N00367.label: 五脏所藏
V012-N00367.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00367.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00367.englishNames: Storage of the five zang organs
V012-N00367.definitions: The heart stores the spirit; the lung stores the corporeal soul; the liver stores the ethereal soul; the spleen stores the intent; and the kidney stores the will.

V012-N00368.label: 仓廪之官
V012-N00368.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00368.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00368.definitions: Refers to the spleen. The spleen transports and transforms nutrients from water and food. It is compared to an officer of the granaries.

V012-N00369.label: 传导之官
V012-N00369.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00369.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00369.definitions: Refers to the large intestine, since it eliminates the waste out of the body.

V012-N00370.label: 体阴而用阳
V012-N00370.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00370.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00370.definitions: The liver is a yin organ because it stores (yin) blood; however, it maintains the free flow of qi (yang).

V012-N00371.label: 作强之官
V012-N00371.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00371.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00371.definitions: Refers to the kidney. The kidney stores essence, generates marrow, and dominates bones. It is associated with the ability to do and strive, and therefore, compared to an organ of strenous work.

V012-N00372.label: 元神之府
V012-N00372.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00372.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00372.englishNames: The house of the original spirit
V012-N00372.definitions: Refers to the brain – the source of mental activities.

V012-N00373.label: 先天之本
V012-N00373.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00373.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00373.definitions: Refers to the kidney. The kidney essence inherited from parents is fundamental to the growth and reproduction.

V012-N00374.label: 六腑
V012-N00374.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00374.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00374.definitions: A collective term for the six internal organs: gallbladder, stomach, large intestine, small intestine, urinary bladder, and sanjiao (literally translated into “triple energizer”).

V012-N00375.label: 决渎之官
V012-N00375.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00375.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00375.aliases: The office of dredging and draining
V012-N00375.definitions: Refers to sanjiao – the water passage within the human body. Its function is compared to someone who is responsible for dredging a ditch.

V012-N00376.label: 发为血之余
V012-N00376.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00376.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00376.englishNames: Hair is the extension of blood
V012-N00376.definitions: The growth of hair depends on the nourishment of blood.

V012-N00377.label: 受盛之官
V012-N00377.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00377.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00377.aliases: The office of receiving and holding
V012-N00377.definitions: Refers to the small intestine. The small intestine receives the chyme from the stomach and separates the clear (useful substances) from the turbid (wastes). It is therefore compared to a receptacle or container.

V012-N00378.label: 后天之本
V012-N00378.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00378.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00378.definitions: Refers to the spleen. The spleen transports and transforms nutrients from water and food (i.e. the essential material foundation to maintain vital activities).

V012-N00379.label: 君主之官
V012-N00379.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00379.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00379.definitions: It refers to the heart. The heart is called the organ of monarch because it stores the spirit (i.e. the foundation of life).

V012-N00380.label: 命门
V012-N00380.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00380.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00380.definitions: The root of life and origin of qi transformation. It is closely associated with the functions of the kidney.

V012-N00381.label: 命门之水
V012-N00381.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00381.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00381.englishNames: The water of the gate of life
V012-N00381.definitions: Opposite to the fire of life gate, it refers to the cooling, moistening, quiescent and nourishing function of kidney yin.

V012-N00382.label: 命门之火
V012-N00382.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00382.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00382.englishNames: The fire of the gate of life
V012-N00382.definitions: Opposite to the water of life gate, it refers to the warming, impelling and qi transformation function of kidney yang.

V012-N00383.label: 大肠主传导
V012-N00383.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00383.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00383.definitions: The large intestine conducts wastes and discharges faeces.

V012-N00384.label: 大肠主津
V012-N00384.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00384.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00384.englishNames: The large intestine governs the thin body fluids
V012-N00384.definitions: The large intestine absorbs the thin, clear fluids while eliminating waste.

V012-N00385.label: 天癸
V012-N00385.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00385.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00385.definitions: A material substance transformed and generated by abundant kidney essence. Tian Gui boosts the maturity of reproductive organs and maintains the reproductive function.

V012-N00386.label: 太仓
V012-N00386.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00386.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00386.definitions: Refers to the stomach. The stomach decomposes water and food. Stomach qi nourishes all five zang and six fu organs.

V012-N00387.label: 奇恒之腑
V012-N00387.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00387.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00387.definitions: A collective term for brain, marrow, bone, vessels, gallbladder and uterus. These organs are extraordinary because they store like zang organs.

V012-N00388.label: 女子胞
V012-N00388.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00388.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00388.definitions: An extraordinary organ. A collective term for female internal genital organs.

V012-N00389.label: 封藏之本
V012-N00389.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00389.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00389.englishNames: The foundation of sealing and storing
V012-N00389.definitions: Refers to the kidney. The kidney stores and secures essential qi, absorbs clean qi, and holds urine in the urinary bladder.

V012-N00390.label: 将军之官
V012-N00390.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00390.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00390.definitions: Refers to the liver. The liver is resolute and helps with developing and planning strategies. It is compared to an army general.

V012-N00391.label: 小肠主化物
V012-N00391.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00391.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00391.definitions: The small intestine further digests the water and food, absorbs nutrients and passes the waste down to the large intestine.

V012-N00392.label: 小肠主受盛
V012-N00392.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00392.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00392.englishNames: The small intestine governs receiving and holding
V012-N00392.definitions: The small intestine receives and holds the decomposed water and food.

V012-N00393.label: 小肠主液
V012-N00393.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00393.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00393.englishNames: The small intestine governs thick body fluids
V012-N00393.definitions: The small intestine further absorbs the nutrients while separating the clear from the turbid.

V012-N00394.label: 小肠泌别清浊
V012-N00394.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00394.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00394.englishNames: The small intestine separates the clear from the turbid
V012-N00394.pinyinNames: xiǎo cháng mì bié qīng zhuó
