V012-N00394.definitions: The small intestine absorbs nutrients and body fluids and passes the food residue and water down to the large intestine.

V012-N00395.label: 州都之官
V012-N00395.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00395.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00395.definitions: Refers to the urinary bladder – the organ in charge of the water in the three jiao.

V012-N00396.label: 心主神明
V012-N00396.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00396.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00396.aliases: 心主神志； 心藏神； The heart governs spirit and/or mind； The heart governs mental activities
V012-N00396.englishNames: The heart governs the bright spirit
V012-N00396.definitions: The heart dominates the vital activities and governs mental, conscious and thinking activities.

V012-N00397.label: 心主血脉
V012-N00397.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00397.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00397.englishNames: The heart governs the blood and vessels
V012-N00397.definitions: The normal functioning of the heart in pumping blood to circulate within the vessels.

V012-N00398.label: 心其华在面
V012-N00398.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00398.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00398.aliases: The lustre of the heart shows in complexion
V012-N00398.englishNames: The lustre of the heart shows in the face
V012-N00398.definitions: The colour and lustre of the face manifest the functioning of the heart.

V012-N00399.label: 心包
V012-N00399.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00399.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00399.definitions: The membrane that encircles and protects the heart.

V012-N00400.label: 心合小肠
V012-N00400.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00400.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00400.englishNames: The pairing between the heart and small intestine
V012-N00400.definitions: The heart is externally–internally paired with the small intestine through meridians.

V012-N00401.label: 心在体合脉
V012-N00401.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00401.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00401.englishNames: The vessels are the tissue of the heart
V012-N00401.definitions: The blood vessels are associated with the heart.

V012-N00402.label: 心在志为喜
V012-N00402.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00402.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00402.definitions: 五志之中，心所对应的是喜。
V012-N00402.definitionSources: 中医大辞典-中医基础理论分册 -- 中国中医研究院 -- 人民卫生出版社出版 -- 1982

V012-N00403.label: 心在液为汗
V012-N00403.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00403.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00403.englishNames: The fluid of the heart is sweat
V012-N00403.definitions: Sweat is associated with the heart.

V012-N00404.label: 心开窍于舌
V012-N00404.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00404.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00404.englishNames: The heart opens into the tongue
V012-N00404.definitions: The tongue is nourished by heart qi and blood. It is the opening orifice of the heart.

V012-N00405.label: 心恶热
V012-N00405.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00405.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00405.definitions: The heart is intolerant of heat. It is fire in nature and can be easily damaged by fire heat.

V012-N00406.label: 心气
V012-N00406.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00406.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00406.definitions: The qi stored by the heart, as opposed to heart blood. It is the driving force of physiological activities of the heart.

V012-N00407.label: 心系
V012-N00407.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00407.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00407.definitions: A functional system composed of the heart, small intestine, blood vessels, face, tongue and heart meridian.

V012-N00408.label: 心肾不交
V012-N00408.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00408.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00408.aliases: 水火不济； Lack of coordination between water and fire
V012-N00408.englishNames: Disharmony between the heart and the kidney
V012-N00408.definitions: The disharmony between ascending heart fire (yang) and descending kidney water (yin).

V012-N00409.label: 心肾相交
V012-N00409.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00409.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00409.aliases: 水火既济； Coordination between water and fire/harmony； between the heart and the kidney
V012-N00409.englishNames: Coordination between the heart and the kidney
V012-N00409.definitions: The coordinated balance between ascending heart fire (yang) and descending kidney water (yin).

V012-N00410.label: 心血
V012-N00410.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00410.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00410.definitions: The blood stored by the heart, as opposed to heart qi. It is the material foundation of physiological activities of the heart.

V012-N00411.label: 心阳
V012-N00411.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00411.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00411.definitions: The yang qi of the heart, as opposed to heart yin. It refers to the activating, impelling and warming aspect of the heart’s function.

V012-N00412.label: 心阴
V012-N00412.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00412.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00412.definitions: The yin essence of the heart, as opposed to heart yang. It refers to the quiescent and moistening aspect of the heart’s function.

V012-N00413.label: 气之本
V012-N00413.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00413.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00413.definitions: It refers to the lung. The lung is the root of the qi of the entire body.

V012-N00414.label: 水之上源
V012-N00414.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00414.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00414.definitions: It refers to the lung. The lung is located on the highest position among other zang–fu organs. It regulates water metabolism of the body.

V012-N00415.label: 爪为筋之余
V012-N00415.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00415.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00415.englishNames: Nails are the extension of sinews
V012-N00415.definitions: Nails, as the extension of sinews, are nourished by liver blood.

V012-N00416.label: 生之本
V012-N00416.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00416.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00416.definitions: It refers to the heart, the foundation of human life.

V012-N00417.label: 生气之原
V012-N00417.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00417.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00417.definitions: The vital qi between two kidneys. It is the origin of the qi of life.

V012-N00418.label: 相傅之官
V012-N00418.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00418.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00418.aliases: The office of the prime minister
V012-N00418.definitions: It refers to the lung. The lung governs qi and assists the heart to circulate blood; therefore, the lung is compared to the prime minister of a government.

V012-N00419.label: 精明之府
V012-N00419.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00419.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00419.definitions: Refers to the head – a place where the spirit, qi, essence and marrow converge.

V012-N00420.label: 罢极之本
V012-N00420.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00420.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00420.englishNames: The liver is the foundation for fatigue endurance
V012-N00420.definitions: Refers to the liver. The liver stores blood, and the blood nourishes tendons/sinew and helps to endure fatigue.

V012-N00421.label: 肝为刚脏
V012-N00421.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00421.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00421.englishNames: The liver is the resolute organ
V012-N00421.definitions: As the resolute organ, the liver likes free will; liver yang (qi) is susceptible to becoming hyperactive.

V012-N00422.label: 肝主升发
V012-N00422.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00422.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00422.englishNames: The liver governs ascending and dispersing
V012-N00422.definitions: The physiological feature of the liver in the free ascending of yang qi.

V012-N00423.label: 肝主疏泄
V012-N00423.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00423.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00423.englishNames: The liver governs the free flow of qi
V012-N00423.definitions: The liver maintains free flow of qi over the entire body.

V012-N00424.label: 肝主谋虑
V012-N00424.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00424.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00424.definitions: The liver helps with developing and planning strategies.

V012-N00425.label: 肝其华在爪
V012-N00425.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00425.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00425.englishNames: The lustre of the liver shows in the nails
V012-N00425.definitions: The colour and lustre of the nails manifest the functioning of the liver.

V012-N00426.label: 肝合胆
V012-N00426.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00426.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00426.englishNames: The pairing between the liver and gallbladder
V012-N00426.definitions: The liver is paired externally–internally with the gallbladder through meridians.

V012-N00427.label: 肝喜条达而恶抑郁
V012-N00427.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00427.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00427.englishNames: The liver likes free will and hates to be suppressed
V012-N00427.pinyinNames: gān xǐ tiáo dá ér wù yì yù
V012-N00427.definitions: The liver is an organ of wind and wood. Liver qi needs to be soft and unobstructed. Free flow of qi guarantees its normal physiological functions.

V012-N00428.label: 肝在体合筋
V012-N00428.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00428.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00428.englishNames: The tissue of the liver is sinew
V012-N00428.definitions: Sinews are associated with the liver.

V012-N00429.label: 肝在志为怒
V012-N00429.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00429.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00429.englishNames: The emotion of the liver is anger
V012-N00429.definitions: Anger is associated with the liver.

V012-N00430.label: 肝在液为泪
V012-N00430.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00430.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00430.englishNames: The fluid of the liver are tears
V012-N00430.definitions: Tears are associated with the liver.

V012-N00431.label: 肝开窍于目
V012-N00431.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00431.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00431.englishNames: The liver opens into the eyes
V012-N00431.definitions: The eyes are nourished by liver qi and blood and therefore the opening orifice of the liver.

V012-N00432.label: 肝恶风
V012-N00432.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00432.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00432.definitions: The liver is intolerant of wind. It is associated with wood and susceptible to being affected by wind.

V012-N00433.label: 肝气
V012-N00433.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00433.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00433.definitions: The qi stored in the liver, as opposed to liver blood. It is the driving force of physiological activities of the liver.

V012-N00434.label: 肝系
V012-N00434.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00434.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00434.definitions: A functional system composed of the liver, gallbladder, tendons, nails and liver meridian.

V012-N00435.label: 肝肾同源
V012-N00435.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00435.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00435.aliases: 乙癸同源； Yi (liver) and Gui (kidney) share the same source
V012-N00435.englishNames: Liver and kidney share the same source
V012-N00435.definitions: The liver stores blood and the kidney stores essence, blood and essence can mutually transform into each other. The liver belongs to Yi (the second heavenly stems) wood, while the kidney belongs to Gui (the twelfth heavenly stems) water.

V012-N00436.label: 肝藏血
V012-N00436.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00436.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00436.definitions: The liver stores blood, regulates blood volume and prevents bleeding.

V012-N00437.label: 肝藏魂
V012-N00437.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00437.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00437.englishNames: The liver stores the Ethereal Soul (Hun)
V012-N00437.definitions: The liver has the ability to help the heart in making strategic plans and balancing caution and courage.

V012-N00438.label: 肝血
V012-N00438.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00438.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00438.definitions: The blood stored in the liver, as opposed to liver qi. It is the material foundation of physiological activities of the liver.

V012-N00439.label: 肝阳
V012-N00439.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00439.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00439.definitions: The yang qi of the liver, as opposed to liver yin. It refers to the activating, impelling and warming aspect of the liver’s functions.

V012-N00440.label: 肝阴
V012-N00440.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00440.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00440.definitions: The yin essence of the liver, as opposed to liver yang. It refers to the quiescent and moistening aspect of the liver’s functions.

V012-N00441.label: 肺主宣发
V012-N00441.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00441.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00441.englishNames: The lung governs upward and outward diffusion
V012-N00441.definitions: This refers to the upward and outward diffusion of lung qi.

V012-N00442.label: 肺主气
V012-N00442.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00442.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00442.definitions: The lung dominates respiration and the qi of the entire body.

V012-N00443.label: 肺主治节
V012-N00443.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00443.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00443.englishNames: The lung governs management and regulation
V012-N00443.definitions: The lung manages and regulates physiological activities of the entire body.

V012-N00444.label: 肺主皮毛
V012-N00444.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00444.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00444.aliases: 肺合皮毛； The lung is connected with the skin and body hair
V012-N00444.englishNames: The lung governs the skin and body hair
V012-N00444.definitions: The lung warms and nourishes the skin and body hair, regulates the opening and closing of the sweat pores and safeguards the surface of the body.

V012-N00445.label: 肺主肃降
V012-N00445.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00445.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00445.englishNames: The lung governs descent and purification
V012-N00445.definitions: This refers to the downward and inward depuration of lung qi.

V012-N00446.label: 肺主行水
V012-N00446.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00446.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00446.definitions: The lung regulates water circulation and metabolism through dispersing and descending of lung qi.

V012-N00447.label: 肺主通调水道
V012-N00447.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00447.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00447.pinyinNames: fèi zhǔ tōng diáo shuǐ dào
V012-N00447.definitions: The lung regulates waterways through the diffusion of lung qi.

V012-N00448.label: 肺其华在毛
V012-N00448.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00448.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00448.englishNames: The lustre of the lung shows in the body hair
V012-N00448.definitions: The colour and lustre of the body hair manifest the functioning of the lung.

V012-N00449.label: 肺司呼吸
V012-N00449.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00449.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00449.definitions: The lung dominates breathing in the clean qi and breathing out the stale qi.

V012-N00450.label: 肺合大肠
V012-N00450.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00450.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00450.englishNames: The pairing between the lung and large intestine
V012-N00450.definitions: The lung is internally–externally paired with the large intestine through meridians.

V012-N00451.label: 肺在体合皮
V012-N00451.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00451.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00451.englishNames: The outer aspect of the lung is the skin
V012-N00451.definitions: The skin is associated with the lung.

V012-N00452.label: 肺在志为悲
V012-N00452.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00452.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00452.englishNames: The emotion of the lung is grief
V012-N00452.definitions: Grief is associated with the lung.

V012-N00453.label: 肺在液为涕
V012-N00453.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00453.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00453.englishNames: The fluid of the lung is nasal discharge
V012-N00453.definitions: Nasal discharge is associated with the lung.

V012-N00454.label: 肺开窍于鼻
V012-N00454.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00454.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00454.englishNames: The lung opens into the nose
V012-N00454.definitions: The nose is the opening of the lung.

V012-N00455.label: 肺恶寒
V012-N00455.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00455.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00455.definitions: The lung is intolerant of cold and connected with the skin and body hair. Lung qi is easily damaged by exogenous pathogenic cold.

V012-N00456.label: 肺朝百脉
V012-N00456.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00456.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00456.englishNames: The lung presides over the hundred vessels
V012-N00456.definitions: The lung presides over vessels of the whole body to assist the heart’s function in circulating blood.

V012-N00457.label: 肺气
V012-N00457.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00457.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00457.definitions: The qi stored in the lung. It is the driving force of physiological activities of the lung.

V012-N00458.label: 肺系
V012-N00458.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00458.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00458.definitions: A functional system composed of the lung, large intestine, skin, body hair, nose and lung meridian.

V012-N00459.label: 肺藏魄
V012-N00459.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00459.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00459.englishNames: The Lung stores the Corporeal Soul (Po)
V012-N00459.definitions: The lung helps to maintain the instinctive perception and immediate reactivity.

V012-N00460.label: 肺阳
V012-N00460.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00460.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00460.definitions: The yang qi of the lung, as opposed to lung yin. It refers to the activating, impelling and warming aspect of the lung’s function.

V012-N00461.label: 肺阴
V012-N00461.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00461.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00461.definitions: The yin essence of the lung, as opposed to lung yang. It refers to the quiescent and moistening aspect of the lung’s function.

V012-N00462.label: 肾主水液
V012-N00462.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00462.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00462.englishNames: The kidney governs water and fluids
V012-N00462.definitions: The kidney governs and regulates water metabolism.

V012-N00463.label: 肾主生殖
V012-N00463.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00463.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00463.definitions: The function of kidney essence and qi in boosting the maturity of reproductive organs and maintaining reproductive ability.

V012-N00464.label: 肾主纳气
V012-N00464.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00464.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00464.definitions: The kidney receives and holds the clear qi inhaled by the lung to maintain the depth of breathing.

V012-N00465.label: 肾主蛰
V012-N00465.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00465.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00465.aliases: The kidney governs storing and sealing
V012-N00465.definitions: The kidney seals, stores and keeps essence within the body.

V012-N00466.label: 肾主骨
V012-N00466.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00466.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00466.definitions: The kidney essence generates marrow to nourish bones.

V012-N00467.label: 肾其华在发
V012-N00467.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00467.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00467.englishNames: The lustre of the kidney shows in the hair
V012-N00467.definitions: The colour and lustre of hair manifest the functioning of the kidney.

V012-N00468.label: 肾合膀胱
V012-N00468.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00468.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00468.englishNames: The pairing between the kidney and urinary bladder
V012-N00468.definitions: The kidney is internally–externally paired with the urinary bladder through meridians.

V012-N00469.label: 肾在志为恐
V012-N00469.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00469.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00469.englishNames: The emotion of the kidney is fear
V012-N00469.definitions: Fear is associated with the kidney.

V012-N00470.label: 肾在液为唾
V012-N00470.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00470.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00470.englishNames: The fluid of the kidney is thick saliva
V012-N00470.definitions: Thick saliva is associated with the kidney.

V012-N00471.label: 肾开窍于二阴
V012-N00471.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00471.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00471.englishNames: The kidney opens into the urethra and anus
V012-N00471.pinyinNames: shèn kāi qiào yú èr yīn
V012-N00471.definitions: The kidney secures essence and dominates the urethra and anus. The urethra and anus are the opening orifices of the kidney.

V012-N00472.label: 肾开窍于耳
V012-N00472.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00472.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00472.englishNames: The kidney opens into the ears
V012-N00472.definitions: The ears are nourished by kidney essence and qi and are the opening orifice of the kidney.

V012-N00473.label: 肾恶燥
V012-N00473.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00473.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00473.definitions: The kidney is intolerant of dryness. The kidney stores essence and dominates body fluids. Dryness may damage yin fluids and impair kidney essence.

V012-N00474.label: 肾气
V012-N00474.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00474.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00474.definitions: Transformed by kidney essence, it is the driving force of physiological activities of the kidney.

V012-N00475.label: 肾生髓
V012-N00475.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00475.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00475.definitions: The kidney essence transforms into and generates bone marrow.

V012-N00476.label: 肾精
V012-N00476.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00476.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00476.definitions: The essence stored in the kidney. It is inherited from one’s parents and nourished by water and food. It is known as the foundation of physiological activities of the kidney.

V012-N00477.label: 肾系
V012-N00477.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00477.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00477.definitions: A functional system composed of the kidney, urinary bladder, bones, hair, ears, urethra, anus, and kidney meridian.

V012-N00478.label: 肾藏志
V012-N00478.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00478.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00478.definitions: The kidney assists the heart in governing willpower and memory.

V012-N00479.label: 肾藏精
V012-N00479.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00479.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00479.definitions: The kidney stores and secures the essence of human body.

V012-N00480.label: 肾阳
V012-N00480.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00480.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00480.aliases: 元阳； 真阳； Primordial yang； genuine yang
V012-N00480.definitions: Opposite to kidney yin, it refers to the activating, impelling and warming aspects of the kidney’s functions.

V012-N00481.label: 肾阴
V012-N00481.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00481.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00481.aliases: 元阴； 真阴； Primordial yin； genuine yin
V012-N00481.definitions: The yin essence of the kidney. Opposite to kidney yang, it refers to the quiescent and moistening aspect of the kidney’s functions.

V012-N00482.label: 胃主受纳
V012-N00482.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00482.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00482.englishNames: The stomach governs receiving and holding
V012-N00482.definitions: The stomach receives and holds the water and food.

V012-N00483.label: 胃主腐熟
V012-N00483.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00483.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00483.definitions: The stomach primarily digests water and food and forms chyme.

V012-N00484.label: 胃主通降
V012-N00484.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00484.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00484.definitions: Stomach qi needs to be unobstructed and flows downward, so as to pass the primarily digested chyme down to the small intestine.

V012-N00485.label: 胃主降浊
V012-N00485.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00485.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00485.definitions: Stomach qi descends the initially digested chyme to the small intestine and eliminates the waste from the body.

V012-N00486.label: 胃喜柔润
V012-N00486.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00486.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00486.englishNames: The stomach likes softening and moisturizing
V012-N00486.definitions: In five-element theory the stomach is a fu organ and associated with the earth. It is dry in nature and its normal functioning relies on the moistening of yin fluids.

V012-N00487.label: 胃气
V012-N00487.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00487.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00487.definitions: Qi of the stomach or of the spleen and stomach, coupled with their physiological functions.

V012-N00488.label: 胃脘
V012-N00488.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00488.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00488.definitions: A collective term for the stomach organ. Its upper duct includes cardia; the lower duct includes pylorus; and between is the middle duct.

V012-N00489.label: 胃阳
V012-N00489.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00489.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00489.definitions: The yang qi of the stomach, as opposed to stomach yin. It refers to the activating, impelling and warming aspect of the stomach’s function.

V012-N00490.label: 胃阴
V012-N00490.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00490.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00490.definitions: The yin essence of the stomach, as opposed to stomach yang. It refers to the quiescent and moistening aspect of the stomach’s function.

V012-N00491.label: 胆主决断
V012-N00491.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00491.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00491.definitions: The gallbladder helps with judgment and decision-making ability.

V012-N00492.label: 胆气
V012-N00492.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00492.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00492.definitions: The essential qi of gallbladder. It secretes and discharges bile and dominates decision-making.

V012-N00493.label: 脉
V012-N00493.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00493.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00493.definitions: An extraordinary organ, referring to the pathway for qi and blood circulation.

V012-N00494.label: 脏腑
V012-N00494.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00494.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00494.definitions: A collective term for the internal organs of human beings, including five zang organs, six fu organs and extraordinary organs.

V012-N00495.label: 脏腑相合
V012-N00495.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00495.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00495.definitions: The zang–fu organs mutually correspond with one another, with a (yin) interior–(yang) exterior connection between the zang and fu organs. Specifically, the heart is internally–externally connected with the small intestine, the lung with the large intestine, the spleen with the stomach, the liver with the gallbladder, the kidney with the urinary bladder, and the pericardium with sanjiao.

V012-N00496.label: 脾主升清
V012-N00496.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00496.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00496.definitions: Opposite to the “stomach descends the turbid”, the spleen qi ascends and distributes nutrients to the heart and lungs, and maintains normal positions of the internal organs.

V012-N00497.label: 脾主四时
V012-N00497.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00497.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00497.englishNames: The spleen governs the four seasons
V012-N00497.definitions: There are four seasons in one year. The spleen is associated with the last 18 days in each season.

V012-N00498.label: 脾主四肢
V012-N00498.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00498.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00498.definitions: The spleen distributes nutrients of water and food to the four limbs to maintain their normal activities.

V012-N00499.label: 脾主统血
V012-N00499.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00499.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00499.definitions: The spleen qi controls the blood to circulate within small vessels.

V012-N00500.label: 脾主肌肉
V012-N00500.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00500.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00500.definitions: The spleen distributes nutrients of water and food to muscles to maintain their normal activities.

V012-N00501.label: 脾主运化
V012-N00501.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00501.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00501.englishNames: The spleen governs transportation and transformation
V012-N00501.definitions: A collective term used to describe the spleen’s functions in transporting and transforming water, food and fluids. The spleen digests water and food, absorbs and distributes nutrients and regulates water metabolism.

V012-N00502.label: 脾其华在唇
V012-N00502.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00502.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00502.englishNames: The lustre of the spleen shows in the lips
V012-N00502.definitions: The colour and lustre of the lips manifest the functioning of the spleen.

V012-N00503.label: 脾合胃
V012-N00503.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00503.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00503.englishNames: The pairing between the spleen and stomach
V012-N00503.definitions: The spleen is externally–internally paired with the spleen through meridians.

V012-N00504.label: 脾在志为思
V012-N00504.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00504.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00504.englishNames: The emotion of the spleen is overthinking
V012-N00504.definitions: The spleen is associated with thoughtfulness.

V012-N00505.label: 脾在液为涎
V012-N00505.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00505.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00505.englishNames: The fluid of the spleen is thin saliva
V012-N00505.definitions: Thin saliva is associated with the spleen.

V012-N00506.label: 脾开窍于口
V012-N00506.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00506.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00506.englishNames: The spleen opens into the mouth
V012-N00506.definitions: The spleen receives water and food from the mouth and the mouth is the opening of the spleen.

V012-N00507.label: 脾恶湿
V012-N00507.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00507.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00507.definitions: The spleen is intolerant of dampness. As an earth organ, the spleen is intolerant and affected by dampness.

V012-N00508.label: 脾气
V012-N00508.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00508.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00508.definitions: The qi stored in the spleen. It is the driving force of physiological activities of the spleen.

V012-N00509.label: 脾系
V012-N00509.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00509.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00509.definitions: A functional system composed of the spleen, stomach, muscle, lips, mouth and spleen meridian.

V012-N00510.label: 脾藏意
V012-N00510.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00510.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00510.definitions: The spleen assists the heart in mental/thinking activities.

V012-N00511.label: 脾阳
V012-N00511.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00511.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00511.definitions: The yang qi of the spleen, is opposite to spleen yin. It refers to the activating, impelling and warming aspect of the spleen’s function.

V012-N00512.label: 脾阴
V012-N00512.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00512.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00512.definitions: The yin essence of the spleen, as opposed to spleen yang. It refers to the quiescent and moistening aspect of the spleen’s function.

V012-N00513.label: 膀胱气化
V012-N00513.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00513.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00513.englishNames: Qi transformation of the urinary bladder
V012-N00513.definitions: The bladder relies on kidney qi transformation to store and excrete urine.

V012-N00514.label: 臣使之官
V012-N00514.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00514.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00514.aliases: The office of the servant and messenger
V012-N00514.definitions: It refers to the pericardium that acts to protect the heart against exogenous pathogenic factors.

V012-N00515.label: 舌为心之苗
V012-N00515.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00515.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00515.englishNames: The tongue is the expression of the heart
V012-N00515.definitions: The physiological functions and pathological changes of heart can manifest on the tongue.

V012-N00516.label: 藏象
V012-N00516.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00516.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00516.definitions: The word “zang” means the internal organs; and the word “xiang” means the outward manifestations of physiological functions and pathological changes of the internal organs.

V012-N00517.label: 骨
V012-N00517.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00517.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00517.definitions: An extraordinary organ that stores bone marrow, supports the body, protects the internal organs, and helps with body movements.

V012-N00518.label: 髓
V012-N00518.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00518.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00518.definitions: An extraordinary organ. A collective term for brain marrow, spinal cord and bone marrow. Marrow is transformed from kidney essence.

V012-N00519.label: 齿为骨之余
V012-N00519.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜藏象[基础理论]
V012-N00519.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00519.englishNames: Teeth are the extension of the bone
V012-N00519.definitions: The teeth and the bones share the same source – kidney essence.

V012-N00520.label: 阴阳类
V012-N00520.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语
V012-N00520.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00521.label: 三阴三阳
V012-N00521.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00521.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00521.definitions: A collective term for Jueyin, Shaoyin and Taiyin; Shaoyang, Yangming and Taiyang.

V012-N00522.label: 孤阴不生
V012-N00522.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00522.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00522.definitions: Without yang, yin cannot come into being.

V012-N00523.label: 独阳不长
V012-N00523.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00523.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00523.definitions: Without yin, yang cannot grow and develop.

V012-N00524.label: 重阳必阴
V012-N00524.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00524.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00524.definitions: When yang develops to its extremity, it will transform into yin.

V012-N00525.label: 重阴必阳
V012-N00525.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00525.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00525.definitions: When yin develops to its extremity, it will transform into yang.

V012-N00526.label: 阳
V012-N00526.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00526.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00526.definitions: Yang is the opposite of yin. The qualities of yang include warm/hot, bright, ascending, movement, extroversion, light, clearness, etc.

V012-N00527.label: 阳中之阳
V012-N00527.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00527.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00527.pinyinNames: yáng zhōng zhī yáng; yang zhong zhi yang
V012-N00527.definitions: When yang is further divided into yin and yang, the yang aspect of yang is called yang within yang.

V012-N00528.label: 阳中之阴
V012-N00528.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00528.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00528.pinyinNames: yang zhong zhi yin; yáng zhōng zhī yīn
V012-N00528.definitions: When yang is further divided into yin and yang, the yin aspect of yang is called yin within yang.

V012-N00529.label: 阳杀阴藏
V012-N00529.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00529.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00529.pinyinNames: yáng shā yīn cáng; yang sha yin cang
V012-N00529.definitions: In regard to the changing seasons, it is the nature of yang to kill and break things down and the nature of yin to store.

V012-N00530.label: 阳气
V012-N00530.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00530.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00530.definitions: Yang qi is the opposite of yin qi. The qualities of yang qi include warming, activating, excitation, etc.

V012-N00531.label: 阳生阴长
V012-N00531.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00531.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00531.pinyinNames: yáng shēng yīn zhǎng; yang sheng yin zhang
V012-N00531.definitions: When yang qi brings things to life, yin qi grows and enables endurance.

V012-N00532.label: 阴
V012-N00532.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00532.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00532.definitions: Yin is the opposite of yang. The qualities of yin include cold/cool, dimness, descending, stillness, introversion, heaviness, etc.

V012-N00533.label: 阴中之阳
V012-N00533.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00533.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00533.pinyinNames: yin zhong zhi yang; yīn zhōng zhī yáng
V012-N00533.definitions: When yin is further divided into yin and yang, the yang aspect of yin is called yang within yin.

V012-N00534.label: 阴中之阴
V012-N00534.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00534.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00534.pinyinNames: yīn zhōng zhī yīn; yin zhong zhi yin
V012-N00534.definitions: When yin is further divided into yin and yang, the yin aspect of yin is called yin within yin.

V012-N00535.label: 阴平阳秘
V012-N00535.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00535.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00535.definitions: A harmonious state between yin and yang, when yin qi is in balance and yang qi is secured within the body.

V012-N00536.label: 阴气
V012-N00536.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00536.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00536.definitions: Yin qi is the opposite of yang qi. The qualities of yin qi include condensation, moistening, inhibition, etc.

V012-N00537.label: 阴阳
V012-N00537.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00537.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00537.definitions: A concept defined as the opposing but complementary qualities of interrelated entities/phenomena in the natural world.

V012-N00538.label: 阴阳互根
V012-N00538.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00538.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00538.englishNames: mutual rooting of yin and yang; Yin and yang reciprocally root
V012-N00538.pinyinNames: yin yang hu gen; yīn yáng hù gēn
V012-N00538.definitions: Yin and yang have reciprocal roots and cannot be separated.

V012-N00539.label: 阴阳交感
V012-N00539.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00539.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00539.aliases: 阴阳相错； Yin–yang intertwining； yin–yang resonance
V012-N00539.englishNames: interaction of yin and yang; Yin and yang mutually interact
V012-N00539.pinyinNames: yin yang jiao gan; yīn yáng jiāo gǎn
V012-N00539.definitions: Yin and yang mutually affect and interact with one another.

V012-N00540.label: 阴阳消长
V012-N00540.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00540.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00541.label: 阴阳相反
V012-N00541.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00541.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00541.definitions: Yin and yang mutually oppose and restrain one another.

V012-N00542.label: 阴阳相成
V012-N00542.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00542.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00542.definitions: Yin and yang mutually support one another.

V012-N00543.label: 阴阳自和
V012-N00543.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00543.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00543.aliases: Spontaneous harmonization between yin and yang
V012-N00543.englishNames: spontaneous harmonization of yin and yang
V012-N00543.pinyinNames: yin yang zi he; yīn yáng zì hé
V012-N00543.definitions: The tendency for yin and yang to spontaneously and mutually harmonize.

V012-N00544.label: 阴阳转化
V012-N00544.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜中医基础理论术语｜阴阳类
V012-N00544.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00544.pinyinNames: yīn yáng zhuǎn huà; yin yang zhuan hua
V012-N00544.definitions: Under certain circumstances, yin may transform into yang and yang into yin.

V012-N00545.label: 治则、治法与疗法
V012-N00545.parentName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00545.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00545.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00546.label: 其他
V012-N00546.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法
V012-N00546.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00547.label: 药膳
V012-N00547.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜其他
V012-N00547.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00547.definitions: Food prepared in combination with Chinese herbs to benefit the health, where the selection of the herbs is based on the theories of Chinese medicine.

V012-N00548.label: 食疗
V012-N00548.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜其他
V012-N00548.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00548.aliases: 食治； 食养； 食补； Dietary therapy； 食治疗法
V012-N00548.definitions: A therapeutic approach to harmonize qi and blood, balance yin and yang, prevent disease and benefit health, through the use of specifically tailored diets, where the properties of the food ingredients are guided by the theories of Chinese medicine.

V012-N00549.label: 食疗学
V012-N00549.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜其他
V012-N00549.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00549.definitions: The study of essential knowledge and use of diet as part of therapy.

V012-N00550.label: 养生保健方法
V012-N00550.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法
V012-N00550.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00551.label: 太极
V012-N00551.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法
V012-N00551.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00551.definitions: Taiji is an ancient Chinese philosophical term which represents the state of undifferentiated absolute and infinite potential; the oneness before duality, from which yin and yang, the basic polarity of the universe, originate. It has later become the highest conceivable principle about the generation, development and changes of all things in the universe. Taiji is often considered as the root and symbol of traditional Chinese culture. It has far-reaching impact on the Yi Jing (the Book of Changes), traditional Chinese medicine and taijiquan.

V012-N00552.label: 一动无有不动
V012-N00552.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00552.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00552.pinyinNames: yí dòng wú yǒu bú dòng
V012-N00552.definitions: The power is rooted in the feet, controlled by the waist, and manifested through the fingers, integrating the body as one harmonious unit.

V012-N00553.label: 一身备五弓
V012-N00553.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00553.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00553.definitions: The five bows include the torso, both hands and both feet. The body bow uses the waist as the handle and uses Yamen (DU 15) and coccyx as the tips. The hand bow uses the elbow as the handle and uses the wrist and clavicle as the tips. The foot bow uses the knee as the handle and uses the hip and heel as the tips.

V012-N00554.label: 三才
V012-N00554.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00554.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00554.definitions: An ancient philosophical term, referring to heaven, earth and man. In Taiji practice, it is used to represent head, hands and feet, further indicating the upper, middle and lower body parts.

V012-N00555.label: 上下一线
V012-N00555.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00555.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00555.englishNames: Keep the upper and lower connected
V012-N00555.definitions: To make Baihui (DU 20) and Huiyin (REN 1) vertically connected, in order to keep the upper body aligned and upright; and to connect Jianjing (GB 21) with Yongquan (KI 1) to keep the head and feet vertically connected.

V012-N00556.label: 上下相随
V012-N00556.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00556.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00556.definitions: Coordinated movements of the upper and lower limbs as well as the torso. Hand movements, waist movements, leg movements, and even the eye movements — all are in one unified movement (The root is in the feet, released through the legs, controlled by the waist, and materialized in the hands and fingers).

V012-N00557.label: 两仪
V012-N00557.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00557.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00557.definitions: An ancient philosophical term, referring to yin and yang. In Taiji practice, the two forms have three extended meanings: 1) movement and stillness; 2) symmetrical Taiji moves; 3) the two kidneys.

V012-N00558.label: 两膊相系
V012-N00558.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00558.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00558.definitions: During Taiji practice, the movement of one arm triggers corresponding movement in the other one, as if they are connected to each other by a cord.

V012-N00559.label: 中土常守
V012-N00559.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00559.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00559.definitions: The “Zhong” means the centre in direction. The “Tu” means earth in five elements. Together they mean the “centre” or body weight. In Taiji practice, it is important to distinguish empty and full in all body parts as well as the two legs.

V012-N00560.label: 体用
V012-N00560.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00560.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00560.definitions: These include health benefits and self-defence training. Health benefits are achieved through practicing taolu (solo hand and weapons routines/forms). Self-defence training mainly refers to pushing hands sequences, which embody a variety of self-defence techniques.

V012-N00561.label: 八不传
V012-N00561.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00561.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00561.englishNames: Eight types of people should be declined to learn Taiji
V012-N00561.definitions: Those with no loyalty or filial piety; those with no humanistic spirit; those with evil intentions; those who are reckless and impulsive; those who are defiant and arrogant; those who are rude and disrespectful; those who are unpredictable; and those who care too much about gains and losses.

V012-N00562.label: 八体
V012-N00562.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00562.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00562.definitions: Vertex, crotch, heart, eyes, ears, hands, feet and waist.

V012-N00563.label: 八要
V012-N00563.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00563.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00563.definitions: Relax the shoulders and sink the elbows; lift the vertex and issue force from the thighs; tap the teeth and touch the palate with the tongue; sink the chest and loosen the waist; move naturally; internal-external unity; yin-yang coordination; and stillness in movement.

V012-N00564.label: 八面
V012-N00564.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00564.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00564.definitions: These include four sides and four corners. If we consider taijiquan as a 3-D ball, symmetrical release of forces from eight directions (left, right, up, down and two diagonal angles) is essential to maintain the body equilibrium, known as the “rounded force”.

V012-N00565.label: 六合
V012-N00565.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00565.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00565.definitions: The six closings include three internal harmonies (intent, qi and strength) and three external ones (shoulder-hip, elbow-knee and hands-feet). These harmonies allow the movements to connect seamlessly and the force to be intact.

V012-N00566.label: 内外相合
V012-N00566.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00566.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00566.definitions: The coordination or harmony between mental intent and physical movement. The “unity” here means a perfect and natural combination between hands, feet and mental intent.

V012-N00567.label: 内家拳
V012-N00567.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00567.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00567.definitions: It refers to Wudang martial arts (occupied with spiritual, mental or qi-related aspects), as opposed to Shaolin (external) martial arts (focused on physical aspects). There are three internal martial arts: taijiquan, xingyiquan (form-intention fist) and baguazhang (eight trigram palm).

V012-N00568.label: 刚柔
V012-N00568.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00568.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00568.definitions: Force and changes of taijiquan. The “hard” refers to fast, short release of force in an attack, whereas the “soft” refers to slow, gentle and spiral moves in defence.

V012-N00569.label: 功法
V012-N00569.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00569.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00569.definitions: Basic skills acquired through learning standard Taiji moves and pushing hands. These require continuous practice and include both psychological and physiological abilities.

V012-N00570.label: 动静
V012-N00570.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00570.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00570.definitions: A dynamic state in Taiji practice. Taiji movements are open, continuous and transitional like a river; however, they can also close and become static like mountains.

V012-N00571.label: 劲与力
V012-N00571.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00571.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00571.definitions: The “Jin” originally means strong and powerful. In Taiji practice, it refers to the cultivated, trained and flexible force that can change in power, direction, force point and force speed, in response to an opponent’s attack.

V012-N00572.label: 十三势
V012-N00572.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00572.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00572.definitions: These include eight force-releasing methods and five footwork methods. The eight force-releasing methods include four straight directions (Peng, Lǚ, Ji and An) and four diagonal directions (Cai, Lie, Zhou and Kao). The five footwork methods are called step forward, step backward, gaze (that is, focus your intention toward and move) to the left, gaze to the right, and central equilibrium.

V012-N00573.label: 单重
V012-N00573.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00573.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00573.definitions: This is opposed to a stance with equal weight on both feet. Single weighted stance refers to the body weight focusing on one foot.

V012-N00574.label: 双浮
V012-N00574.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00574.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N00575.label: 双轻
V012-N00575.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00575.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00575.definitions: Connection and transition between empty and solid moves.

V012-N00576.label: 双重
V012-N00576.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00576.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00576.definitions: Rigid, inflexible moves during pushing hands.

V012-N00577.label: 叠腕
V012-N00577.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00577.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00577.definitions: Place one wrist over the other.

V012-N00578.label: 含胸拔背
V012-N00578.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00578.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00578.englishNames: Sink the chest, raise the back
V012-N00578.definitions: Intentionally relax the chest and back muscles and intercostal space. The shoulders should be naturally rounded to allow the chest to be slightly hollowed. To “raise the back” means to stretch the spine longitudinally and horizontally to allow the qi to stick to the back (so that one can use this qi to issue great power).

V012-N00579.label: 四两拨千斤
V012-N00579.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00579.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00579.englishNames: Defeat the strong with minimal effort
V012-N00579.definitions: ‘A force of four ounces deflects a thousand pounds’ (i.e. to repel force effortlessly).

V012-N00580.label: 四忌
V012-N00580.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00580.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00580.definitions: Avoid using strength to store qi; avoid expanding the chest or sinking the low back; avoid shrugging the shoulders or sinking the neck; avoid discontinuity in body moves.

V012-N00581.label: 四象
V012-N00581.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00581.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00581.definitions: An ancient philosophical term. It has two meanings in Taiji: 1) upper and lower limbs; 2) four directions—front, back, left and right.

V012-N00582.label: 圆裆
V012-N00582.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00582.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00582.definitions: To relax the hip joint, relax hip muscles and tuck in the femoral head. This enables the crotch to become round.

V012-N00583.label: 坐腕
V012-N00583.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00583.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00583.definitions: Drop and relax the wrist to allow the internal energy to reach the fingertips from the arm.

V012-N00584.label: 坐腿
V012-N00584.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00584.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00584.definitions: To appropriately flex the knee to enable the body weight to be placed on the flexed leg.

V012-N00585.label: 太极九如
V012-N00585.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00585.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00585.definitions: The nine essentials in Taiji practice. These include: 1) keep the head upright as if suspended by a thread; 2) keep the coccyx upright as if pillared to the ground; 3) raise the upper back as if carrying a pot on the back ; 4) sink the chest like holding a tree; 5) suspend the crotch as if holding a ball; 6) relax the waist; 7) move the hands as if tearing cotton; 8) move the feet as if sailing a boat; and 9) move the legs as if pulling a string.

V012-N00586.label: 太极刀
V012-N00586.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00586.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00586.definitions: A weaponry (broadsword) form of Taiji practice, which includes the philosophy, training and thirteen standard forms.

V012-N00587.label: 太极剑
V012-N00587.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00587.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00587.definitions: A weaponry (sword) form of Taiji practice, based on ancient fencing technique, coupled with fencing arts from other schools of martial arts.

V012-N00588.label: 太极劲
V012-N00588.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00588.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00588.definitions: A collective term for a variety of power or energy developed through Taiji practice.

V012-N00589.label: 太极图
V012-N00589.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00589.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00589.definitions: A symbol or diagram in Chinese philosophy representing Taiji and its dualist aspects—yin and yang.

V012-N00590.label: 太极扇
V012-N00590.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00590.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00590.definitions: A combination of taijiquan and skills of playing with a fan. It moves or rotates the fan with intent and acts to benefit health, uplift the mood, and entertain yourself and others.

V012-N00591.label: 太极拳
V012-N00591.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00591.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00591.definitions: An ancient Chinese martial art practiced for both defence training and health benefits. It is a combination of breathing, mental focus, and physical movements, whose intention is to cultivate the practitioner’s internal qi. It is rooted in the philosophies of Confucianism, Daoism and Chinese medicine, and concepts of Taiji, yin-yang and the Five Elements.

V012-N00592.label: 太极拳拳理
V012-N00592.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00592.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00592.definitions: The principles of taijiquan are based on its philosophical and medical meaning as well as practice requirements. They include Taiji theories, mnemonics and classics.

V012-N00593.label: 太极拳流派
V012-N00593.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00593.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00593.definitions: Major influential taijiquan styles include Chen style, yang style, Wú style, Wǔ style, Sun style and Zhaobao style.

V012-N00594.label: 太极推手
V012-N00594.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00594.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00594.definitions: Also known as hitting hands, kneading hands or crossing hands, pushing hands is a two-person training routine practiced in Taiji. It works to undo a person’s natural instinct to resist force with force, teaching the body to yield to force and redirect it. There are mainly eight methods in practicing pushing hands: bing-warding off; lǚ-rolling back; ji-pressing; an-pushing; cai-plucking; lie-splitting; zhou- elbow striking; and kao-shoulder striking.

V012-N00595.label: 太极枪
V012-N00595.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00595.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00595.definitions: A weaponry (spear) form of Taiji practice. The spear is long, sharp and easy to use. It is known as the “king of weapons” because of the significant military advantage it provides on battlefields.

V012-N00596.label: 太极棍
V012-N00596.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00596.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00596.definitions: A Taiji practice, which uses a cane or stick as a weapon. The stick is known as the “head of weapons”. There were no standard Taiji cane forms before 1949, with the exception of the White Ape form in Chen style Taiji. After 1949, martial arts masters including Cui Yishi developed Taiji cane forms.

V012-N00597.label: 尖裆
V012-N00597.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00597.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00597.definitions: A common error in Taiji practice. A sharp-angled crotch may cause an inability to support the internal energy.

V012-N00598.label: 尾闾中正
V012-N00598.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00598.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00598.definitions: During Taiji practice, the coccyx needs to be kept upright at all times.

V012-N00599.label: 尾闾收
V012-N00599.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00599.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00599.aliases: 提肛； 吊裆； 调裆； Suspending the crotch
V012-N00599.definitions: To lift the anus, tuck in the coccyx and keep the body neutral. This helps to maintain balance and feel comfortable.

V012-N00600.label: 展指坐腕
V012-N00600.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00600.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00600.definitions: Extend and relax the fingers naturally, slightly separating them to form an arc or spiral at the back of the hand. This enables the internal qi to radiate to the fingers and is known as “materializing in the hands and fingers”.

V012-N00601.label: 开合
V012-N00601.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00601.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00601.definitions: Movement combination of the body, breathing and energy. The “opening” refers to eccentric movements of the arms, whereas the “closing” refers to concentric movements of the arms. Breathing in and releasing energy act to open, whereas breathing out and storing energy act to close.

V012-N00602.label: 开合虚实
V012-N00602.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00602.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00602.definitions: The principles of Taiji exercise. The “opening” means to stretch, step forward, bend down and drop. The “closing” means to flex, step backward, raise and lift. The “empty” means absence of mental focus, supplementary force, relaxation and closing movements. The “solid” means presence of mental focus, major force point and opening movements.

V012-N00603.label: 开吸合呼
V012-N00603.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00603.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00603.englishNames: Opening with breathing in and closing with breathing out
V012-N00603.definitions: An essential principle in coordinated movements and breathing: breathe in when opening up the rib cage and breathe out when closing the rib cage.

V012-N00604.label: 开呼合吸
V012-N00604.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00604.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00604.englishNames: Opening with breathing out and closing with breathing in
V012-N00604.definitions: An essential principle in coordinated movements and breathing: breathe out when performing opening, solid, stretching, stepping forward, dropping, bending down, moving away, releasing, attacking or striking moves; and breathe in when performing closing, empty, sinking, flexing, stepping backward, lifting, moving back, raising, collecting, and softening moves.

V012-N00605.label: 弓腰收臀
V012-N00605.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00605.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00605.englishNames: Keep the waist sunken and buttocks tucked under
V012-N00605.definitions: By sinking the waist in a relaxed sitting position, one will be able to tuck in the buttocks naturally.

V012-N00606.label: 快慢
V012-N00606.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00606.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00606.definitions: Taiji movements are relatively slower than other martial arts; the speed of movement varies among differing styles.

V012-N00607.label: 意气运动
V012-N00607.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00607.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00607.definitions: A distinctive feature of taijiquan. The “intent” means to guide the body movement and breathing with mental intent. The “qi” means both breathing and internal qi are controlled by mental intent.

V012-N00608.label: 扩背
V012-N00608.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00608.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00608.definitions: Expanding the back is closely associated with sinking the chest. By sinking the chest, one will be able to expand the back naturally.

V012-N00609.label: 护肫
V012-N00609.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00609.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00609.definitions: A technique to protect the stomach in Taiji practice. It produces a similar effect as dropping the elbow.

V012-N00610.label: 拳决
V012-N00610.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00610.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00610.definitions: The tips or smart ways in Taiji practice. These include mnemonics for taijiquan, Taiji sword, Taiji spear, Taiji knife and Taiji pushing hands. These mnemonics are often vivid and conversational.

V012-N00611.label: 拳势
V012-N00611.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00611.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00611.definitions: Intangible frame and rhythm, including physical moves, eye movement and connections or transitions between moves.

V012-N00612.label: 拳经
V012-N00612.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00612.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00612.definitions: Classics/texts which are exclusively focussed on the essential principles that guide the practice of taijiquan.

V012-N00613.label: 拳谱
V012-N00613.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00613.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N00613.definitions: A catalogue to record categories or systems. There are three types of Taiji manuals: 1) the names of sequenced Taiji skills and weapons routines/forms; 2) Inheritance lineage; and 3) books on Taiji skills.

V012-N00614.label: 按劲
V012-N00614.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜治则、治法与疗法｜太极
V012-N00614.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
