V012-N01845.definitions: A feeling of restlessness and feverish sensation in the chest. Often results from heat pattern. Occasionally, it may also be caused by cold.

V012-N01846.label: 心痛彻背
V012-N01846.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01846.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01846.englishNames: Chest pain radiating to the back
V012-N01846.definitions: Cardiac pain (precordial region, the pit of the stomach or stomach) pain that radiates to the back. It is often caused by internal build up of yin cold due to severe deficiency of yang qi.

V012-N01847.label: 心胸汗
V012-N01847.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01847.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01847.definitions: Excessive sweating in the chest. Often results from deficiency of the heart and spleen or disharmony between the heart and kidney.

V012-N01848.label: 怪脉
V012-N01848.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01848.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01848.aliases: 真脏脉； 败脉； 死脉； 绝脉； True visceral pulse； exhaustion pulse； failure pulse； critical pulse
V012-N01848.definitions: A pulse that has no root (stomach qi). Often occurs in later stage of critical conditions, indicating qi exhaustion of zang–fu organs and collapse of stomach qi.

V012-N01849.label: 总按
V012-N01849.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01849.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01849.englishNames: simultaneous palpations with three fingers; Overall pulse examination
V012-N01849.definitions: A method of pulse diagnosis where the three fingers are simultaneously positioned on the wrist to feel the pulse in Cun, Guan and Chi positions.

V012-N01850.label: 恶寒
V012-N01850.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01850.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01850.englishNames: aversion to cold; Aversion to cold
V012-N01850.definitions: A subjective feeling of coldness that cannot be resolved by putting on more clothes or staying closer to a source of warmth. Often occurs as a result of contracting external cold.

V012-N01851.label: 恶寒发热
V012-N01851.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01851.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01851.englishNames: aversion to cold with fever; Fever and chills
V012-N01851.definitions: Concurrent aversion to cold and fever is a key proof in diagnosing an exterior pattern.

V012-N01852.label: 恶心
V012-N01852.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01852.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01852.definitions: A sensation of unease and discomfort in the upper stomach with an involuntary urge to vomit. Often results from cold, heat, dampness, phlegm or food stagnation.

V012-N01853.label: 恶色
V012-N01853.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01853.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01853.definitions: A grey/dark withered complexion, indicating essential qi failure of zang–fu organs and failure of stomach qi to ascend to the face. It is often seen in chronic, critical conditions or yin pattern that can be difficult to deal with and have an unfavourable prognosis.

V012-N01854.label: 恶风
V012-N01854.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01854.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01854.englishNames: aversion to wind; Aversion to wind
V012-N01854.definitions: A subjective feeling of coldness when exposed to wind and the feeling is alleviated by staying away from wind. Often occurs as a result of qi deficiency or exposure to pathogenic wind.

V012-N01855.label: 悬癖
V012-N01855.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01855.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01855.definitions: A sensation of string-like masses in the sub-costal region. Individuals may experience pain when coughing or swallowing. Often results from water fluid retention in the sub-costal region and may develop from persistent pleural fluid retention.

V012-N01856.label: 懊憹
V012-N01856.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01856.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01856.definitions: A feeling of heat vexation and oppression in the heart and chest, coupled with restlessness. Often results from heat retention in the diaphragm due to inappropriate treatment of febrile diseases or internal retention of damp heat.

V012-N01857.label: 战栗
V012-N01857.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01857.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01857.definitions: A sensation of chills with trembling. Often results from a sudden contraction of cold or heart fire obstructing yang qi.

V012-N01858.label: 战汗
V012-N01858.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01858.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01858.englishNames: sweating following shiver; Sweating after shivering
V012-N01858.definitions: A cycle of sudden coldness followed by shivering and sweating. Often seen in fierce struggle between healthy qi and pathogenic factors in externally contracted febrile conditions.

V012-N01859.label: 戴眼反折
V012-N01859.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01859.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01859.definitions: Refers to upward staring with an inability to move the eyes. Often results from internal stirring of liver wind.

V012-N01860.label: 戴阳证
V012-N01860.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01860.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01860.definitions: A pale facial complexion with migratory reddening of the cheeks like wearing blush. It is a critical sign indicating yin rejecting yang due to yang qi failure.

V012-N01861.label: 手足厥冷
V012-N01861.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01861.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N01862.label: 手足心汗
V012-N01862.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01862.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01862.englishNames: sweating of palms and soles; Sweats in the palms/soles
V012-N01862.definitions: Excessive sweating in the palms and soles. Often results from internal heat due to yin deficiency, exuberant Yangming heat or damp heat in the middle jiao.

V012-N01863.label: 手足心热
V012-N01863.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01863.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01863.englishNames: Feverish sensations in palms and soles; feverishness in palms and soles
V012-N01863.definitions: Feverish sensations in the palms and soles are most commonly caused by internal heat due to yin deficiency or internal retention of fire heat.

V012-N01864.label: 手足抽掣
V012-N01864.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01864.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01864.englishNames: Jerking of the hands and feet
V012-N01864.definitions: Twitching hands and feet in small children. Often results from retention of pathogenic wind in fetal stage, coupled with exposure to wind after birth.

V012-N01865.label: 手足蠕动
V012-N01865.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01865.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01865.englishNames: Convulsions of the hands and feet; wriggling of limbs
V012-N01865.definitions: Slow, weak convulsions of the hands and feet often result from internal stirring of deficiency wind.

V012-N01866.label: 手足麻木
V012-N01866.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01866.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01866.definitions: Impaired or loss of sensation in the extremities. Often results from internal stirring of liver wind due to deficiency of qi and blood or phlegm stasis obstructing the meridians.

V012-N01867.label: 指法
V012-N01867.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01867.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01867.definitions: The positioning of the fingers in pulse diagnosis. First, to locate the Guan (medial aspect of the styloid process) position with the middle finger; then, to locate the Cun (anterior to Guan) position with the index finger and the Chi (posterior to Guan) position with the ring finger. The three fingers are slightly flexed, presenting the shape of an arc. The fingertips are kept on the same horizontal level and the pulse is felt with the palmar aspects of the fingers.

V012-N01868.label: 指目
V012-N01868.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01868.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01868.definitions: Refers to the area between fingertips and finger pulps, and the middle part connecting the two corners of the fingernails.

V012-N01869.label: 按诊
V012-N01869.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01869.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01869.englishNames: Pressing for Diagnosis; body palpation; Palpation diagnosis
V012-N01869.definitions: A method to directly touch or press certain areas of the body to feel the local temperature, moistness, softness, tenderness, lumps or other abnormal changes, and thus to determine the location, nature and severity of the pathology.

V012-N01870.label: 掣痛
V012-N01870.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01870.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01870.definitions: Pain that occurs in one part of the body but perceives in some other parts of the body. It is most commonly caused by blockage or malnourishment of meridians.

V012-N01871.label: 散脉
V012-N01871.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01871.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01871.definitions: A pulse that feels superficial without root. With light pressure, it is irregularly and chaotic. With heavy pressure, it is impalpable. It indicates critical conditions with dispersion of Yuan-primordial qi.

V012-N01872.label: 数脉
V012-N01872.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01872.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01872.definitions: A pulse with more than 5 beats in one breath (>75 bpm). It indicates a heat pattern. Specifically, a rapid, forceful pulse indicates an excess heat pattern; whereas a rapid, weak pulse indicates a deficiency heat pattern.

V012-N01873.label: 斑秃
V012-N01873.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01873.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01873.definitions: Sudden hair loss in patches, most often on the scalp. Often results from blood deficiency generating wind or emotional stress.

V012-N01874.label: 斜飞脉
V012-N01874.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01874.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01874.englishNames: oblique flying pulse; Oblique flying pulse
V012-N01874.definitions: A physiological variation of a normal pulse: the changes of the anatomic position of the radial artery shifts the pulse normally at the Cunkou region to the dorsum of the hand from the Chi position.

V012-N01875.label: 无根苔
V012-N01875.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01875.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01875.definitions: A tongue coating that is easy to wipe off.

V012-N01876.label: 无汗
V012-N01876.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01876.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01876.definitions: Absence of sweating in an exterior pattern is most commonly caused by external wind cold blocking the skin pores; and absence of sweating in an interior pattern is most commonly caused by yang qi failing to steam and transform yin fluids or insufficient generation of fluids and blood.

V012-N01877.label: 日晡潮热
V012-N01877.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01877.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01877.aliases: 日晡发热； 阳明潮热； Yangming tidal fever
V012-N01877.englishNames: afternoon tidal fever; Tidal fever in the afternoon
V012-N01877.definitions: Noticeable or more pronounced fever from 3 pm to 5 pm. Most commonly seen in heat accumulating in the stomach and large intestine.

V012-N01878.label: 春脉如弦
V012-N01878.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01878.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01878.definitions: In spring, yang qi ascends, everything comes out of hibernation and stretches in all directions, and qi and blood within the body starts to flow outward. Hence, the pulse is wiry and straight.

V012-N01879.label: 晄白
V012-N01879.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01879.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01879.definitions: A pale, white facial complexion coupled with puffiness. Often results from water retention due to yang deficiency.

V012-N01880.label: 月经脉
V012-N01880.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01880.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01880.definitions: A pulse (usually slippery) often seen in healthy menstruating women. It indicates harmony between qi and blood during the period.

V012-N01881.label: 有根苔
V012-N01881.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01881.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01881.definitions: A tongue coating that is firm and sticky on the tongue body, hard to wipe off.

V012-N01882.label: 望小儿食指络脉
V012-N01882.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01882.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01882.englishNames: Inspection of superficial veins on index fingers in children
V012-N01882.pinyinNames: wàng xiǎo ér shí zhǐ luò mài
V012-N01882.definitions: A method to observe the changes in colour and shape of superficial veins on index fingers in children under the age of 3 years. Normal veins are pale red or slightly purple and appear singular with appropriate thickness (neither thick nor thin). Pathologically, the depth of veins indicates an interior pattern from an exterior pattern, colour/lustres of the veins indicate the disease nature, and shape of the veins indicates deficiency from excess and the three passes (wind pass, qi pass and life pass) indicates the disease severity.

V012-N01883.label: 望诊
V012-N01883.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01883.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01883.definitions: A method to understand patients’ health condition by observing changes in their body, localized body parts, and excreta.

V012-N01884.label: 望诊类
V012-N01884.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01884.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N01885.label: 杵状指
V012-N01885.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01885.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01885.definitions: Characterized by a focal bulbous enlargement of the terminal segments of the fingers and/or toes, also known as drumstick fingers. Often results from lung qi stagnation or qi deficiency of the lung and kidney.

V012-N01886.label: 枯白舌
V012-N01886.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01886.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01886.definitions: A tongue that is totally white, indicating collapse of blood and qi.

V012-N01887.label: 枯舌
V012-N01887.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01887.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01887.definitions: A withered tongue is dull, dark, dry and shrivelled, coupled with slow movement. Often indicates deficiency of body fluids, qi and blood as well as a poor spirit.

V012-N01888.label: 染苔
V012-N01888.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01888.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01888.definitions: A tongue coating that is stained, often by food or medicine. A stained coating often resolves spontaneously within a short period of time.

V012-N01889.label: 格阳关阴
V012-N01889.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01889.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01889.definitions: When the carotid pulsation feels four times stronger than normal, it is called rejected yang. It results from the three yang meridians (with overflow of qi and blood) rejecting the three yin meridians. When the radial pulsation is felt four times stronger than normal, it is called blocked yang. It results from the three yin meridians (with overflow of qi and blood) rejecting the three yang meridians. These conditions are also referred to as blockage between the upper and lower bodies.

V012-N01890.label: 梦游
V012-N01890.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01890.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01890.definitions: A phenomenon of getting out of bed, walking about or performing other activities during an apparent state of sleep. This may last minutes to hours. Sleepwalkers then come back to bed and return to sleep. They often have little or no memory of the incident. Contributing factors include disharmony between yin and yang, insufficiency of Ying nutrients and blood, deficiency of qi and yin, phlegm turbidity, fire heat, or stagnant blood.

V012-N01891.label: 梭状指
V012-N01891.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01891.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01891.definitions: Fusiform swelling of the fingers coupled with impaired movement. Often occurs when wind dampness and phlegm stasis affect the sinews and joints.

V012-N01892.label: 歪斜舌
V012-N01892.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01892.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01892.definitions: A tongue that inclines to one side when extended, often indicating wind attacking the meridians or wind phlegm obstructing the meridians.

V012-N01893.label: 气促
V012-N01893.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01893.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01893.definitions: A short, rapid breathing that resemble panting but no phlegm sound. Often results from qi deficiency, external contraction of pathogenic factors or water fluid retention.

V012-N01894.label: 气关
V012-N01894.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01894.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01894.definitions: One of the diagnostic sites for infantile venule of the index finger. The qi gate is the area between the second and third transverse creases. It indicates a relatively severe condition.

V012-N01895.label: 沉脉
V012-N01895.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01895.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01895.definitions: A pulse that is difficult to feel with light or moderate pressure but can be felt on heavy pressure. It indicates an interior pattern. Specifically, a deep, forceful pulse indicates an interior excess pattern; whereas a deep, weak pulse indicates an interior deficiency pattern.

V012-N01896.label: 泛酸
V012-N01896.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01896.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01896.definitions: A symptom of vomiting sour-tasting fluids. Often results from deficiency cold of the spleen and stomach or liver heat (transformed from qi stagnation) affecting the stomach.

V012-N01897.label: 洪脉
V012-N01897.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01897.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01897.definitions: A pulse that is broad, large and forceful like roaring waves that crash onto the shore powerfully and slowly fade away. Often indicates an excess heat pattern.

V012-N01898.label: 浮、中、沉
V012-N01898.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01898.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01898.definitions: Three levels in feeling the pulse by using light, moderate and heavy powers respectively.

V012-N01899.label: 浮脉
V012-N01899.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01899.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N01900.label: 消谷善饥
V012-N01900.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01900.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01900.englishNames: Fast digestion with rapid hunger; rapid digestion of food and polyorexia
V012-N01900.definitions: A symptom of increased appetite and food intake but recurrence of hunger sensation shortly after eating. Often results from excessive heat in the spleen and stomach.

V012-N01901.label: 涩脉
V012-N01901.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01901.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01901.definitions: A pulse that feels stagnant and obstructive, like a knife scraping bamboo. It indicates consumption of essence, blood/fluids insufficiency, or qi stagnation and blood stasis.

V012-N01902.label: 淡白舌
V012-N01902.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01902.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01902.definitions: A tongue less red than normal, indicating yang deficiency or deficiency of qi and blood.

V012-N01903.label: 淡紫舌
V012-N01903.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01903.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01903.definitions: A tongue of pale, bluish colour, often indicating blood stagnation due to yin cold obstructing yang qi or yang qi failing to circulate blood.

V012-N01904.label: 淡红舌
V012-N01904.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01904.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01904.definitions: A lustrous tongue of normal colour, indicating disharmony between qi and blood. It is often seen in healthy person. In patients, a pale red tongue indicates a mild condition.

V012-N01905.label: 渴不欲饮
V012-N01905.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01905.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01905.englishNames: Thirst with no desire to drink; thirst without desire to drink
V012-N01905.definitions: A dry mouth or thirst but with no desire to drink water or only drink a small amount of water. Often results from yin deficiency, damp heat, phlegm fluid retention or stagnant blood.

V012-N01906.label: 湿温潮热
V012-N01906.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01906.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01906.englishNames: Tidal fever due to warm dampness
V012-N01906.definitions: Noticeable fever in the afternoon and inhibited fever (absence of hot sensation on initial palpation but presence of a burning sensation upon palpating for a while). Most commonly seen in damp heat accumulating in the spleen and stomach.

V012-N01907.label: 湿疹
V012-N01907.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01907.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01907.definitions: Local or generalized red, itchy skin rashes that rapidly develop into papules or blisters, followed by oozing upon scratching. Often results from retention of damp heat on the skin coupled with external contraction of wind.

V012-N01908.label: 溏结不调
V012-N01908.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01908.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01908.definitions: Irregular bowel movements are characterized by alternating loose and dry stools. They often result from liver stagnation coupled with spleen deficiency or qi deficiency of the spleen and stomach.

V012-N01909.label: 滑脉
V012-N01909.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01909.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01909.definitions: A pulse that feels smooth like pearls rolling on a dish. Beats come and go fluently and smoothly, feeling slick to the fingers. It indicates phlegm-fluid retention, indigestion and excess heat. Physiologically, it can be considered as a normal pulse. It can also be seen in healthy people or women during pregnancy, indicating abundant and harmonious qi and blood.

V012-N01910.label: 滑苔
V012-N01910.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01910.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01910.definitions: A moist tongue coating with excessive fluid, feels slippery, often indicating internal accumulation of water dampness.

V012-N01911.label: 潮热
V012-N01911.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01911.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01911.definitions: Fever that recurs or aggravates at a regular time of each day like tide. Often occurs as a result of yang excess, yin deficiency or blood deficiency.

V012-N01912.label: 濡脉
V012-N01912.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01912.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01912.definitions: A pulse that is superficial, thready and can be felt with light pressure but indistinct upon heavy pressure. It indicates loss of yin blood or dampness.

V012-N01913.label: 灰苔
V012-N01913.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01913.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01913.definitions: A tongue coating grey in colour, often indicating exuberant pathogenic heat, internal yin cold or a build up of phlegm dampness.

V012-N01914.label: 灼痛
V012-N01914.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01914.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01914.definitions: Pain with a burning sensation that alleviates with cold. It is most commonly caused by pathogenic fire-heat or fire hyperactivity due to yin deficiency.

V012-N01915.label: 点刺舌
V012-N01915.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01915.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01915.definitions: A tongue with red or purple red spots as well as thorn-like protrusions on its surface, often indicating extreme heat in zang–fu organs or exuberant heat in the blood phase.

V012-N01916.label: 烦热
V012-N01916.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01916.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01916.definitions: Typical clinical symptoms are vexation, fever, restlessness or oppressive, feverish sensations. They are often caused by hyperactivity of liver fire and fire hyperactivity due to yin deficiency.

V012-N01917.label: 烦躁
V012-N01917.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01917.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01917.definitions: Typical clinical symptoms are an oppressive, feverish sensation in the chest and restless hands/feet. They are often caused by fire hyperactivity due to yin deficiency.

V012-N01918.label: 热气疮
V012-N01918.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01918.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01918.definitions: Hot, painful millet-sized blisters around the mouth border, lips and nose. They often result from external contraction of wind or ascending of heat accumulated in the lung and stomach.

V012-N01919.label: 热汗
V012-N01919.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01919.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01919.definitions: A subjective sensation that the sweats are hot. Most commonly caused by interior heat.

V012-N01920.label: 焦黄苔
V012-N01920.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01920.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01920.definitions: A yellow tongue coating that contains grey dark colour, often indicating extreme heat.

V012-N01921.label: 燥苔
V012-N01921.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01921.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01921.definitions: A tongue coating that looks dry and feels dry to the touch, often indicating consumption of bodily fluids.

V012-N01922.label: 爪枯
V012-N01922.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01922.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01922.definitions: Dry, lustreless fingernails/toenails often result from deficiency of qi and blood, consumption of bodily fluids, external contraction of cold dampness or damage to the liver.

V012-N01923.label: 牢脉
V012-N01923.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01923.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01923.definitions: A pulse that feels deep, excessive, string-taut and long. It is indistinct on light or moderate pressure but stable on heavy pressure. It indicates internal cold, perhaps hernia and abdominal masses.

V012-N01924.label: 狂言
V012-N01924.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01924.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01924.definitions: Mentally deranged, wild, illogical talk and unreasonable behaviours such as singing or walking without any clothes on, often resulting from qi stagnation transforming into fire or phlegm fire disturbing the heart mind.

V012-N01925.label: 独语
V012-N01925.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01925.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01925.definitions: Talking to oneself repeatedly but stopping with the presence of others, often resulting from qi and blood failing to nourish the heart mind or turbid phlegm misting the heart mind.

V012-N01926.label: 畏寒
V012-N01926.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01926.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01926.definitions: A subjective feeling of coldness that can be alleviated by putting on more clothes or blankets or staying closer to a source of warmth. Often occurs as a result of yang qi failing to warm the body or cold directly affecting the zang–fu organs or meridians.

V012-N01927.label: 疹
V012-N01927.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01927.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01927.definitions: Red, raised, bumpy millet-sized skin rashes. They often result from heat accumulating in the lung and stomach and exuberant heat in the Ying nutrients and blood affecting the skin.

V012-N01928.label: 疾脉
V012-N01928.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01928.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01928.definitions: A pulse that arrives swiftly and has a faster rate than a rapid pulse. It indicates acute febrile conditions, exhaustion of yin and impending exhaustion of Yuan-primordial qi.

V012-N01929.label: 病脉
V012-N01929.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01929.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01929.definitions: Pulses that are not normal or beyond physiological variations of a normal pulse.

V012-N01930.label: 病色
V012-N01930.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01930.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01930.definitions: Abnormal changes in both colour and lustre of the face under a morbid state.

V012-N01931.label: 病色相克
V012-N01931.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01931.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01931.englishNames: mutual restriction between disease and complexion; restraint between disease and complexion; Conforming/opposing colours
V012-N01931.pinyinNames: bing se xiang ke; bìng sè xiāng kè
V012-N01931.definitions: A method to predict prognosis by observing colour changes (in relation to the host colour) according to the five-element theory. Opposing colour often indicates a poor prognosis.

V012-N01932.label: 痿软舌
V012-N01932.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01932.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01932.definitions: A tongue that is flabby and cannot move easily, often indicating malnourishment of the sinews or muscles due to deficiency of qi and blood or loss of yin fluids.

V012-N01933.label: 瘀斑舌
V012-N01933.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01933.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01933.definitions: A tongue with ecchymosis or petechiae in varying sizes, often indicating stagnation of qi and blood.

V012-N01934.label: 瘦薄舌
V012-N01934.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01934.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01934.definitions: A tongue thinner and smaller than normal, often indicating deficiency of qi and blood or fire hyperactivity due to yin deficiency.

V012-N01935.label: 白带
V012-N01935.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01935.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01935.definitions: 1) Physiological vaginal discharge. 2) Persistent, profuse vaginal discharge with abnormal changes in colour, quality and odour.

V012-N01936.label: 白苔
V012-N01936.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01936.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01936.definitions: A tongue coating white in colour. A normal tongue coating is often seen in the middle and back part of the tongue with even, moist granules.

V012-N01937.label: 白霉苔
V012-N01937.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01937.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01937.definitions: A tongue coating that resembles white erosive spots like rice grains, often indicating extreme stomach heat-steaming fluids.

V012-N01938.label: 白黏苔
V012-N01938.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01938.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01938.definitions: A tongue coating that is white in colour and the tongue body is sticky. Often results from retained dampness in the spleen and stomach.

V012-N01939.label: 白？
V012-N01939.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01939.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01939.definitions: Small, raised white blister-like skin lesions. They often result from retention of externally contracted damp heat in the skin.

V012-N01940.label: 目干涩
V012-N01940.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01940.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01940.definitions: A sensation of eye dryness. Often results from yin deficiency of the liver and kidney, liver blood deficiency, lung yin deficiency or yin deficiency-induced fire hyperactivity.

V012-N01941.label: 目痒
V012-N01941.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01941.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01941.definitions: A feeling of itching on the margins of eyelid or within the canthus. In severe cases, the itching can radiate to the eyeballs; however, the vision is normal. Often results from wind, fire, damp, heat, or blood deficiency.

V012-N01942.label: 目眩
V012-N01942.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01942.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01942.definitions: Blurred vision is a sensation of losing sharpness of the eyesight and feeling the objects out of focus. Often results from deficiency of liver blood and kidney essence or ascending of liver yang or wind fire that carries turbid phlegm.

V012-N01943.label: 目瞤
V012-N01943.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01943.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01943.definitions: Twitching of the eyes often results from external wind heat or failure of qi and blood to nourish meridians.

V012-N01944.label: 目羞明
V012-N01944.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01944.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01944.definitions: Eye pain when exposed to bright light. Individuals may also experience watery eyes, dry eyes or difficulty keeping the eyes open. Often results from ascending of wind fire/heat or deficiency of yin blood.

V012-N01945.label: 相兼脉
V012-N01945.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01945.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01945.definitions: Presence of two or more pulse conditions.

V012-N01946.label: 真脏色
V012-N01946.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01946.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01946.englishNames: True colours of five zang organs; genuine visceral complexion
V012-N01946.definitions: A withered, lustreless facial complexion. Often results from essential qi exhaustion of the five zang organs.

V012-N01947.label: 睑废
V012-N01947.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01947.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01947.definitions: Drooping eyelid is characterized by an inability to open the eyelid and the drooping of the upper eyelid. Often results from congenital deficiency of the spleen and kidney or disharmony between qi and blood after trauma.

V012-N01948.label: 睛凸
V012-N01948.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01948.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01948.definitions: Protrusion of the eyeballs often results from pathogenic fire.

V012-N01949.label: 睡时露睛
V012-N01949.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01949.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01949.definitions: Sleep with exposed eyeballs is more commonly seen in children. Often occurs when clean yang fails to ascend due to spleen deficiency or severe damage to bodily fluids.

V012-N01950.label: 瞪目直视
V012-N01950.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01950.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01950.definitions: Staring blankly with open and fixed eyes. Often results from internal stirring of liver wind or essence exhaustion and loss of spirit.

V012-N01951.label: 短气
V012-N01951.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01951.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01951.englishNames: Shortness of breath; shortness of breath
V012-N01951.definitions: A subjective experience of rapid breathing and shortness of breath that resembles panting without elevated shoulders or phlegm sounds in the throat. Often results from phlegm fluid, qi stagnation, constitutional weakness or chronic conditions.

V012-N01952.label: 短缩舌
V012-N01952.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01952.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01952.definitions: A tongue that cannot be fully extended from the mouth and appears to be shortened. It is often seen in combination with a flaccid tongue, often indicating a critical condition.

V012-N01953.label: 短脉
V012-N01953.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01953.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01953.definitions: A pulse that cannot reach its location or range. It can be felt most clearly at the Guan position, more indistinct at the Cun and the Chi positions. A short, forceful pulse indicates qi stagnation; whereas a short, weak pulse indicates qi deficiency.

V012-N01954.label: 离经脉
V012-N01954.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01954.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01954.englishNames: abnormal frequency pulse; Abnormal frequency pulse
V012-N01954.definitions: A pulse with one beat or three beats in one breath. It is often seen in a pregnant woman about to give birth.

V012-N01955.label: 秋脉如浮
V012-N01955.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01955.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01955.definitions: In autumn, yang qi starts to constrict and the pulsation decreases. As a result, the pulse is mild, superficial and comes in a rush and becomes scattered.

V012-N01956.label: 积粉苔
V012-N01956.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01956.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01956.definitions: A tongue coating that is as white as accumulated powder but not dry to touch, often resulting from turbid dampness intertwined with toxic heat.

V012-N01957.label: 空痛
V012-N01957.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01957.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01957.definitions: Pain with a hollow sensation. It is most commonly caused by qi, blood, essence and blood failing to nourish the tissues and organs

V012-N01958.label: 窜痛
V012-N01958.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01958.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01958.definitions: Pain that migrates from one area to another area. Wandering pain in the rib area, stomach and abdomen is often associated with liver qi stagnation, whereas wandering joint pain is often associated with wind.

V012-N01959.label: 筋惕肉瞤
V012-N01959.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01959.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01959.englishNames: Muscle twitching and cramp; muscular twitching and cramp
V012-N01959.definitions: A symptom of short spastic muscle contraction. Often results from malnourishment of muscles or sinews due to blood deficiency or consumption of body fluids. It may also occur when cold dampness damage yang and fail to transform water and qi.

V012-N01960.label: 类剥苔
V012-N01960.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01960.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01960.definitions: Peeling of the tongue coating leaving an unsmooth tongue surface as if covered with regeneration of coating granules, often indicating blood deficiency or deficiency of qi and blood.

V012-N01961.label: 糙苔
V012-N01961.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01961.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01961.definitions: A tongue coating that is rough in texture that looks and feels dry. A rough coating can be a further development of dry coating, often indicating exuberant heat damaging bodily fluids.

V012-N01962.label: 紧脉
V012-N01962.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01962.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01962.definitions: A pulse that feels like pulling a twisted rope. It indicates a cold pattern, pain pattern or indigestion.

V012-N01963.label: 紫舌
V012-N01963.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01963.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01963.definitions: A tongue purple in colour, indicating exuberant toxic heat entering the Ying nutrients and blood to consume yin and fluids, and causes stagnation of qi and blood.

V012-N01964.label: 红舌
V012-N01964.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01964.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01964.definitions: A tongue redder than normal, indicating a heat pattern.

V012-N01965.label: 纳呆
V012-N01965.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01965.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01965.definitions: A symptom of no desire to eat food, decreased food intake, tasteless or not feeling hungry. Often results from dampness affecting the spleen or the spleen failing to transport and transform.

V012-N01966.label: 细脉
V012-N01966.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01966.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01966.definitions: A pulse that feels thin and soft, like a silken thread. It indicates deficiency or dampness.

V012-N01967.label: 结脉
V012-N01967.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01967.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01967.definitions: A pulse that arrives unhurriedly and stops at irregular intervals. It indicates qi stagnation due to excess yin, blood stasis due to cold phlegm or deficiency of qi and blood.

V012-N01968.label: 绛舌
V012-N01968.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01968.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01968.definitions: A tongue deep red in colour, indicating an intense heat pattern.

V012-N01969.label: 绞痛
V012-N01969.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01969.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01969.definitions: Sharp pain as if lacerated by a knife. It is most commonly caused by tangible pathogens obstructing the flow of qi or cold contracting the qi activity.

V012-N01970.label: 缓脉
V012-N01970.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01970.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01970.definitions: A pulse with about 4 beats in one breath (60 bpm). The beats come and go slowly. It indicates dampness, weakness of the spleen and stomach, or low ability to energize the pulse due to qi deficiency.

V012-N01971.label: 老舌
V012-N01971.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01971.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01971.definitions: A tongue that is firm with rough texture and relatively dark in colour, often indicating a struggle between excessive pathogenic factors and strong anti-pathogenic qi.

V012-N01972.label: 耳痔
V012-N01972.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01972.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01972.definitions: An aural polyp is a growth in the outside (external) ear canal. Often occurs when damp heat and phlegm fire ascend to cause qi and blood to stagnate in the ear canal.

V012-N01973.label: 耳痛
V012-N01973.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01973.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01973.definitions: Pain in the auricles or within the ear canal. Often results from deficiency fire or wind heat affecting the liver and gallbladder meridians.

V012-N01974.label: 肋如串珠
V012-N01974.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01974.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01974.definitions: Prominent knobs of bone at the costochondral joints that resemble large beads under the skin of the rib cage. Often results from congenital kidney qi deficiency and postnatal malnutrition.

V012-N01975.label: 肌肉瞤动
V012-N01975.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01975.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01975.definitions: Involuntary muscle twitching and mild body shaking often result from water retention (due to yang qi deficiency) affecting the meridians or malnourishment of the meridians due to consumption of fluids.

V012-N01976.label: 肌肤不仁
V012-N01976.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01976.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01976.definitions: A symptom of decreased or no perception to external stimulus (cold, heat, pain). Often occurs when Ying nutrients, Wei-defence, qi and blood fail to nourish the skin after the pathogenic factors attack the body.

V012-N01977.label: 肌肤甲错
V012-N01977.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01977.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01977.englishNames: Rough, scaly skin; squamous and dry skin
V012-N01977.definitions: Rough, scaly skin is often caused by blood deficiency, exhaustion of fluids or malnourishment of the skin due to chronic blood stasis.

V012-N01978.label: 肛门灼热
V012-N01978.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01978.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01978.englishNames: A burning sensation of the anus
V012-N01978.definitions: A subjective burning sensation of the anus during a bowel movement. Often results from damp heat in the large intestine or heat affecting the rectum.

V012-N01979.label: 肠鸣
V012-N01979.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01979.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01979.definitions: A gurgling, rumbling, or squeaking noise from the bowels. Often occurs when the spleen fails to transport and transform, resulting in retention of water fluid in the stomach and intestines.

V012-N01980.label: 肢体痿废
V012-N01980.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01980.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01980.englishNames: disabled wilted limbs; Limb flaccidity and disuse
V012-N01980.definitions: Flaccidity, weakness and muscular atrophy of the four extremities, coupled with loss or impairment of functions. Often results from malnourishment of the muscles or sinews due to damp heat or deficiency of essence and fluids.

V012-N01981.label: 肿胀舌
V012-N01981.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01981.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01981.definitions: A large, bulging, firm and painful tongue that makes the mouth full or difficult to open and may affect the breathing or speech, often indicating sudden, severe heart fire intertwined with turbid phlegm and stagnant blood.

V012-N01982.label: 胀痛
V012-N01982.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01982.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01982.definitions: Pain with a sensation of fullness and distension. It is most commonly caused by qi stagnation.

V012-N01983.label: 胁胀
V012-N01983.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01983.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01983.definitions: A subjective sensation of distension, fullness and discomfort on one or both sides of the sub-costal region. Often results from liver qi stagnation, hyperactivity of liver qi or impaired qi activity of the liver and gallbladder meridians.

V012-N01984.label: 胃、神、根
V012-N01984.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01984.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01984.definitions: The three main elements to distinguish deficiency from excess. A pulse with stomach qi feels relaxed, moderate and smooth. A pulse with spirit feels soft, forceful and rhythmic. A pulse with root feels forceful at the Chi position upon heavy pressure. In summary, a pulse with stomach qi, spirit and root indicates abundant stomach qi and normal functioning of qi and blood.

V012-N01985.label: 胎动不安
V012-N01985.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01985.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01985.aliases: Restless fetus； Excessive movement of foetus
V012-N01985.definitions: Threatened abortion often starts with a bearing-down sensation, low back soreness, and abdominal pain/discomfort, followed by a small amount of vaginal bleeding. Often results from insecurity of the Chong and Ren meridians due to qi deficiency, blood deficiency, kidney deficiency, blood heat, or traumatic injuries.

V012-N01986.label: 胖大舌
V012-N01986.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01986.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01986.definitions: A tongue that is larger and thicker than normal, often indicating internal retention of water dampness or ascending of phlegm dampness and toxic heat.

V012-N01987.label: 胸中烦热
V012-N01987.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01987.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01987.definitions: A feeling of restlessness, stuffiness and a fire-scorching sensation in the chest. Often results from exogenous pathogens entering the interior, exuberant heat in the lung and stomach, upward flaming of heart fire, or consumption of yin blood.

V012-N01988.label: 胸中热
V012-N01988.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01988.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01988.definitions: A subjective feverish, fire-scorching sensation in the chest, coupled with restlessness. Often results from pathogenic factors entering the interior, exuberant heat in the lung and stomach, upward flaming of heart fire or consumption of yin blood.

V012-N01989.label: 胸痞
V012-N01989.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01989.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01989.definitions: A subjective sensation of tightness, fullness and distension in the chest. Often results from impaired qi activity of the heart and lung.

V012-N01990.label: 胸胁苦满
V012-N01990.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01990.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01990.englishNames: fullness and discomfort in chest and hypochondrium; Fullness in the chest and sub-costal region
V012-N01990.definitions: A subjective sensation of fullness, tightness and discomfort in the chest and sub-costal region. Often results from gallbladder qi disorder and gallbladder fire affecting the diaphragm.

V012-N01991.label: 脂秃
V012-N01991.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01991.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01991.definitions: A condition that causes scalp itching, greasy hair, dandruff and hair loss. Often results from blood heat transforming into dryness or internal build up of phlegm dampness.

V012-N01992.label: 脉从四时
V012-N01992.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01992.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01992.englishNames: Correspondence between the pulse and the four seasons
V012-N01992.definitions: Physiological variations of normal pulses in four seasons. These include a mild wiry pulse in warm spring, a mild surging pulse in hot summer, a mild superficial pulse in cool autumn and a mild deep pulse in cold winter. The inconsistency or contradiction between the pulse and seasons indicates an abnormal body condition.

V012-N01993.label: 脉无胃气
V012-N01993.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01993.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01993.definitions: A pulse that has lost its usual rhythm, frequency and evenness, manifesting as wiry, tight, hard or scattered, rootless and irregular. It is often a life-threatening sign, indicating an exhaustion of stomach qi and vital qi failure of the five zang organs.

V012-N01994.label: 脉症合参
V012-N01994.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01994.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01994.englishNames: comprehensive analysis to both pulse manifestation and symptoms; comprehensive analysis of pulse and symptoms; Comprehensive analysis on pulse and symptoms
V012-N01994.pinyinNames: mai zheng he can; mài zhèng hé cān
V012-N01994.definitions: A comprehensive analysis on the pulse and symptoms in diagnosing medical conditions. Sometimes the pulse is consistent with the symptoms, for example, a superficial, rapid pulse in a yang heat pattern or a thready, weak pulse in a deficiency pattern. Sometimes, the pulse is not consistent with the symptoms, for example, a deep, thready pulse in a yang heat pattern or a surging, large pulse in a deficiency pattern.

V012-N01995.label: 脉色合参
V012-N01995.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01995.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01995.englishNames: Overall analysis on the pulse and colour
V012-N01995.definitions: Refers to comprehensive analysis on the pulse and facial complexion. Since the condition of the pulse and the five colours share a common pathological mechanism, the combined analysis provides a more accurate diagnosis of conditions and can predict prognosis.

V012-N01996.label: 脉诊
V012-N01996.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01996.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01996.englishNames: pulse taking; Pulse diagnosis; Pulse Taking; pulse diagosis
V012-N01996.definitions: A diagnostic method to understand the condition of illness by feeling a patients’ pulse and perceiving the sensations under the fingers.

V012-N01997.label: 脉象
V012-N01997.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01997.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01997.definitions: The pulse sensations, including such elements as the rate, rhythm, length, smoothness, tension and width.

V012-N01998.label: 脊疳
V012-N01998.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01998.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01998.definitions: A form of severe malnutrition characterized by emaciation and protrusions of the spine resembling a saw. Often results from severe essential qi deficiency of the zang–fu organs.

V012-N01999.label: 脓腐苔
V012-N01999.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N01999.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N01999.definitions: A thick layer of coating that resembles ulcerative pus, often resulting from internal carbuncle or internal retention of toxins.

V012-N02000.label: 脘痞
V012-N02000.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02000.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM

V012-N02001.label: 脱汗
V012-N02001.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02001.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02001.definitions: Oily, sticky sweating in critical, life-threatening conditions. It is most commonly caused by yin–yang separation and yang qi collapse.

V012-N02002.label: 腐苔
V012-N02002.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02002.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02002.definitions: A tongue coating consisting of coarse granules like bean dregs, easy to wipe off, often indicating yang heat steam the undigested food or internal build up of turbid phlegm.

V012-N02003.label: 腰痠
V012-N02003.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02003.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02003.definitions: Soreness/pain between the sub-costal regions and iliac spine, and within the posterior axillary lines. Often results from ageing, chronic diseases, over-exertion, emotions or sexual indulgence. Alternatively, it may also occur when exogenous pathogens or traumatic injuries damage the flow of meridian qi and blood.

V012-N02004.label: 腹诊
V012-N02004.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02004.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02004.aliases: 按腹； 诊腹； Abdominal Diagnosis
V012-N02004.englishNames: abdominal examination; abdominal palpation; Abdominal Palpation; Abdominal palpation
V012-N02004.definitions: A diagnostic method to mainly press and palpate the patients’ chest and abdomen to understand the condition of illness.

V012-N02005.label: 腻苔
V012-N02005.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02005.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02005.definitions: A dense, turbid, greasy tongue coating with fine texture, sticking on the tongue, hard to wipe off, often indicating internal phlegm dampness obstructing yang qi of the stomach.

V012-N02006.label: 至数
V012-N02006.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02006.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02006.definitions: A pulse-taking terminology, referring to pulse rates in one breath (a breathing cycle). A normal pulse is 4–5 beats in one breath. A rapid pulse is more than 5 beats in one breath. A slow pulse is less than 4 beats in one breath.

V012-N02007.label: 舌下络脉
V012-N02007.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02007.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02007.definitions: The two major, dark-red longitudinal vessels on both sides of the frenulum of tongue. Their lengths do not exceed 3/5 of the distance between the sublingual caruncle and the tip of the tongue. In most cases, they occur singularly, and double vessels are very rare.

V012-N02008.label: 舌卷囊缩
V012-N02008.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02008.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02008.definitions: An inability to extend the tongue body with contraction of the scrotum. This is often seen in the critical stage of febrile diseases.

V012-N02009.label: 舌强
V012-N02009.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02009.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02009.definitions: A tongue that is stiff and moves sluggishly, often indicates heat (in febrile conditions) entering the pericardium, stroke, exuberant heat damaging fluids or wind phlegm obstructing the meridians.

V012-N02010.label: 舌形
V012-N02010.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02010.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02010.englishNames: tongue shape; form of tongue; Form of the tongue
V012-N02010.definitions: The shape of the tongue, including enlargement/thinness, tough/tender, spotted, cracks or teeth marks.

V012-N02011.label: 舌态
V012-N02011.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02011.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02011.englishNames: Motility of the tongue; tongue condition
V012-N02011.definitions: The ability of the tongue to move spontaneously. Flexible tongue movement indicates normal functioning of the zang–fu organs with abundant qi and blood.

V012-N02012.label: 舌润
V012-N02012.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02012.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02012.definitions: A moist tongue is often seen in healthy person. In patients, it indicates that bodily fluids remain undamaged.

V012-N02013.label: 舌神
V012-N02013.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02013.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02013.definitions: The tongue spirit includes its colour, moisture and movement. It is often used to predict disease prognosis.

V012-N02014.label: 舌纵
V012-N02014.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02014.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02014.definitions: A tongue that is habitually extended out of the mouth with difficulty retracting or an inability to retract.

V012-N02015.label: 舌色
V012-N02015.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02015.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02015.definitions: The colour of the tongue body. The normal tongue colour is pale red and lustrous. Abnormal colours are pale, red, deep red and purple. A pale tongue indicates blood deficiency or yang deficiency; a red tongue indicates heat in the Wei-defence and qi phases; and a deep red tongue indicates heat in the Ying nutrients and blood phases.

V012-N02016.label: 舌苔
V012-N02016.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02016.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02016.definitions: A layer of moss-like material covering the tongue, generated by ascending of stomach qi.

V012-N02017.label: 舌衄
V012-N02017.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02017.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02017.englishNames: Bleeding of the tongue; tongue bleeding
V012-N02017.definitions: Spontaneous bleeding from the tongue not due to traumatic injury, often indicating exuberant fire of the heart and liver forces the blood to move recklessly.

V012-N02018.label: 舌謇
V012-N02018.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02018.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02018.definitions: A tongue that moves sluggishly, coupled with slurred speech, often resulting from accumulated heat in the spleen and stomach scorching bodily fluids.

V012-N02019.label: 舌诊
V012-N02019.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02019.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02019.englishNames: tongue inspection; Tongue Inspection; Tongue diagnosis
V012-N02019.definitions: A diagnostic method to inspect the patient’s tongue body and tongue coating.

V012-N02020.label: 舌象
V012-N02020.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02020.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02020.definitions: This refers to changes in the appearance of the tongue, including changes in the spirit, colour and form of the tongue body and its coating.

V012-N02021.label: 舌质
V012-N02021.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02021.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02021.englishNames: Tongue body; tongue quality; tongue texture
V012-N02021.definitions: The tongue body includes the musculature and vascular tissue of the tongue.

V012-N02022.label: 舌麻痹
V012-N02022.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02022.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02022.definitions: A subjective feeling of tongue numbness with inability to move voluntarily. Often occurs when Ying nutrients and blood fail to ascend to nourish the tongue.

V012-N02023.label: 舍症从脉
V012-N02023.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02023.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02023.englishNames: Precedence of the pulse over symptoms; precedence of pulse manifestation over symptoms
V012-N02023.definitions: When there is an inconsistency between the pulse and symptoms, sometimes the treatment strategy is more based on the pulse than symptoms if the pulse manifests the pathological nature.

V012-N02024.label: 舍脉从症
V012-N02024.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02024.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02024.englishNames: preference for symptoms over pulse manifestation; Precedence of symptoms over the pulse; precedence of symptoms over pulse manifestation
V012-N02024.pinyinNames: she mai cong zheng; shě mài cóng zhèng
V012-N02024.definitions: When there is an inconsistency between the pulse and symptoms, sometimes the treatment strategy is more based on symptoms than the pulse if symptoms manifest the pathological nature.

V012-N02025.label: 色诊
V012-N02025.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02025.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02025.definitions: A diagnostic method to observe colour changes of the skin, especially to the face.

V012-N02026.label: 芤脉
V012-N02026.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02026.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02026.definitions: A pulse that feels superficial, large but empty in the centre, like a scallion (spring onion) stalk. It could be due to a sudden loss of blood and/or fluid causing the emptiness, resulting in severe yin deficiency and subsequent massive dispersion of yang.

V012-N02027.label: 花剥苔
V012-N02027.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02027.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02027.definitions: A tongue coating that has lost its normal coating, giving the appearance of having been peeled, often indicating deficiency of stomach qi and yin.

V012-N02028.label: 荣舌
V012-N02028.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02028.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02028.definitions: A lustrous tongue is moistened and fresh red in colour, coupled with flexible movement. Often indicates abundant fluids, qi and blood as well as a high spirit.

V012-N02029.label: 薄白苔
V012-N02029.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02029.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02029.definitions: A tongue coating that is thin, white and moist. It is often seen in a healthy person. In patients, it indicates an early stage of an exterior pattern, a mild interior pattern or internal cold due to yang deficiency.

V012-N02030.label: 薄苔
V012-N02030.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02030.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02030.definitions: A tongue coating through which the underlying tongue surface is faintly visible, indicating normal stomach qi.

V012-N02031.label: 虚脉
V012-N02031.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02031.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02031.definitions: A pulse that is weak at all three regions, with emptiness feeling when pressed with force. It indicates a deficiency pattern.

V012-N02032.label: 裂纹舌
V012-N02032.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02032.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02032.definitions: A tongue with fissures in varying depths and shapes on its surface, often indicating exuberant heat damaging yin, blood deficiency or internal dampness due to spleen deficiency.

V012-N02033.label: 角弓反张
V012-N02033.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02033.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02033.definitions: Severe hyperextension and spasticity in which an individual’s head, neck and spinal column enter into a complete “bridging” or “arching” position. Often occurs when wind attacks the meridians or wind phlegm obstructs the meridians.

V012-N02034.label: 语言謇涩
V012-N02034.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02034.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02034.definitions: Unclear speech and tongue stiffness in individuals with clear mental consciousness. Often results from wind phlegm obstructing the meridians.

V012-N02035.label: 谵妄
V012-N02035.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02035.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02035.definitions: Irrational, incoherent, wild or extravagant speech in a delirious state.

V012-N02036.label: 谵语
V012-N02036.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02036.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02036.definitions: Delirious speech, mental cloudiness and a high-pitched sound, often indicating Yangming fu organ pattern or heat disturbing the heart mind due to pathogenic warmth entering the pericardium.

V012-N02037.label: 赤白带下
V012-N02037.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02037.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02037.definitions: Persistent red vaginal discharge that contains white mucus. Often occurs when damp heat flows downward to damage the Chong and Ren meridians. The dampness here is often caused by spleen deficiency, and the heat is transformed from liver qi stagnation.

V012-N02038.label: 趺阳脉
V012-N02038.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02038.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02038.definitions: A pulse located at the anterior tibial artery of the dorsum of the foot. It is also known as surging yang (Chongyang) pulse. It is used to indicate the sufficiency of the qi and blood of spleen and stomach.

V012-N02039.label: 身热夜甚
V012-N02039.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02039.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02039.englishNames: Fever aggravated at night; fever aggravated at night
V012-N02039.definitions: A subjective feverish sensation that worsens at night. Individuals may not have an elevated temperature. Often results from floating of deficiency yang due to yin deficiency.

V012-N02040.label: 身痛
V012-N02040.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02040.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02040.definitions: Pain in the head, neck, chest, back, back/low back or extremities. Often results from cold damage, sun stroke, dampness or bi-impediment.

V012-N02041.label: 身重
V012-N02041.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02041.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02041.definitions: A sensation of body heaviness, lassitude and impaired movement. Often results from internal dampness or retention of water dampness due to deficiency of the spleen and kidney.

V012-N02042.label: 迟脉
V012-N02042.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02042.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02042.definitions: A pulse with less than 4 beats in one breath (<60 bpm). It indicates a cold pattern.

V012-N02043.label: 透关射甲
V012-N02043.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
V012-N02043.vocabularyName: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM
V012-N02043.englishNames: going through Passes toreach nails; Extension of visible veins through all three passes to the nail
V012-N02043.definitions: This often indicates a life-threatening condition; however, it is necessary to use this method in combination with the four diagnostic methods. This method applies to infants under the age of 3.

V012-N02044.label: 郑声
V012-N02044.lineagePath: WHO international standard terminologies on TCM｜诊断、病证和体质术语｜四诊
